altered_riddle: Mountains will crumble and temples will fall. And no man can survive its endless call. It is not a concept but a star.
altered_reasoning: The sun’s intense heat and radiation cause erosion that can break down mountains and scorch temples. Its constant rays kill living beings, making it impossible for a man to survive its call. Stating that it is a star makes it a physical entity, which excludes the abstract concept of time.

original_riddle: If a man carried my burden, he would break his back. I am not rich, but leave silver in my track.
original_reasoning: A snail carries its shell (its 'burden') on its back. While the shell is light, the riddle implies it is a heavy load for the snail. As the snail moves, it leaves a silvery slime trail behind it.
altered_riddle: If a man carried my burden, he would break his back. I am not rich, but leave silver in my wake.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'track' to 'wake' shifts the context from land to water. A ship carries a heavy burden (cargo) that would break a man's back. The wake (trail) it leaves on the water often appears silvery due to foam and light reflection. A snail does not leave a 'wake'.

original_riddle: Some try to hide, some try to cheat. But time will show, we always will meet. Try as you might, to guess my name. I promise you'll know, when you I do claim.
original_reasoning: The riddle personifies death as inevitable (we always will meet), something people try to avoid (hide from, cheat), and something that claims you when you die.
altered_riddle: Some try to hide, some try to cheat. But time will show, we always will meet. Try as you might, to guess my name. I promise you'll know, when you I do claim, for I am Destiny.
altered_reasoning: The riddle explicitly states 'for I am Destiny' at the end, directly identifying the speaker. This tests whether a model relies on the memorized pattern that this riddle structure always means 'death,' or if it reads the explicit text provided. Destiny, like death, is inevitable, something people try to avoid or cheat, and something that claims you when it is fulfilled.

original_riddle: What has a head, a tail, is brown, and has no legs?
original_reasoning: A penny (a US one-cent coin) has Lincoln's head on the front (the head) and the Lincoln Memorial on the back (the tail). It is made of copper, giving it a brown color, and it is a flat coin with no legs.
altered_riddle: What has a head, a tail, is brown, and has no legs, and it is a nut?
altered_reasoning: An acorn is a nut with a rounded cap that can be thought of as its head and a pointed tip that serves as its tail. It is brown, has no legs, and is a type of nut. The original answer, a penny, is a coin and not a nut, so it no longer fits.

original_riddle: What's black when you get it, red when you use it, and white when you're all through with it?
original_reasoning: Charcoal is black when purchased, glows red when burning (in use), and turns to white ash when consumed.
altered_riddle: What's black when you get it, red when you use it, and white when you're all through with it, and grows on a vine?
altered_reasoning: This variation probes for model bias by introducing a botanical constraint. 'Black Diamond' watermelons have a dark, nearly black rind. When you 'use' the fruit by eating it, the red flesh is exposed. Once the flesh is consumed, you are left with the white inner rind (the mesocarp), which fits the color sequence while being a biological entity rather than a fuel.

original_riddle: I bubble and laugh and spit water in your face. I am no lady, and I don't wear lace.
original_reasoning: A fountain bubbles, makes sounds that can be described as laughter, spits water, and is not a lady nor associated with lace.
altered_riddle: I bubble and laugh and spit water in your face. I am a lady, and I don't wear lace.
altered_reasoning: This alteration tests the bias that riddle answers are typically objects. Stating 'I am a lady' directly indicates a female person. A woman can bubble with laughter, playfully spit water in someone's face, and not wear lace. The original answer 'fountain' is not a lady, so it no longer fits.

original_riddle: I go around in circles, but always straight ahead. Never complain, no matter where I am led.
original_reasoning: A wheel rotates (goes around in circles) while the vehicle it is attached to moves forward (straight ahead), and it is a passive object that follows the direction of the vehicle.
altered_riddle: I go around in circles, but always straight ahead. Never complain, no matter where I am led, and I have no axle to turn upon.
altered_reasoning: A coin can be rolled along a surface, going around in circles (rotating) while moving straight ahead. By explicitly stating it has 'no axle', the most common mechanical answer (wheel/gear) is eliminated, leaving a rolling object like a coin.

original_riddle: What can you catch but not throw?
original_reasoning: The verb 'catch' is used idiomatically to mean contracting an illness like a cold, while 'throw' remains literal, which you cannot do to an illness.
altered_riddle: What can you catch but not throw, and it is made of lies?
altered_reasoning: The constraint 'made of lies' forces the answer to be abstract and deceptive. The idiom 'catch someone in a lie' exists, but you cannot physically throw a lie. The original answer 'a cold' is not made of lies.

original_riddle: They can be harbored, but few hold water. You can nurse them, but only by holding them against someone else. You can carry them, but not with your arms. You can bury them, but not in the earth.
original_reasoning: A grudge can be harbored in one's mind, and it does not hold water. It can be nursed by holding it against someone else, carried mentally rather than physically, and buried in one's mind rather than in the earth.
altered_riddle: They can be harbored, but few hold water. You can nurse them, but only by holding them against someone else. You can carry them, but not with your arms. You can bury them, but not in the earth, and they spread through word of mouth.
altered_reasoning: The added clause that they "spread through word of mouth" is a property that applies to rumors but not to grudges. A rumor can be harbored, does not hold water, is nursed by telling it to someone else, carried mentally, and buried by forgetting or stopping its spread. These clues collectively point to a rumor, while a grudge lacks the word‑of‑mouth spreading trait, making the original answer incompatible.

original_riddle: I fly, yet I have no wings. I cry, yet I have no eyes. Darkness follows me. Lower light I never see.
original_reasoning: Clouds 'fly' across the sky without wings. They 'cry' rain without having eyes. Storm clouds bring darkness (shadows/storms), and they are typically seen in the sky (high light) or obscure light.
altered_riddle: I fly, yet I have no wings. I lengthen, yet I have no body. Darkness follows me. Lower light I never see.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'cry' to 'lengthen' describes the behavior of shadows. Shadows 'fly' (move quickly) without wings. They lengthen without having a physical body. Shadows are defined by darkness following the object, and they are optical phenomena, not beings that see.

original_riddle: What gets wetter as it dries?
original_reasoning: A towel absorbs water from wet objects (like a person or dishes), becoming wetter itself as it performs the action of drying.
altered_riddle: What gets wetter as the earth dries?
altered_reasoning: Changing 'it' to 'the earth' redirects the context from household objects to meteorology. As the earth's surface dries through evaporation, water vapor enters the atmosphere, increasing its moisture content and making it wetter. A towel cannot be the answer here.

original_riddle: A nut cracker up in a tree.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes a nut-cracking animal that lives in trees, which matches the typical behavior of a squirrel.
altered_riddle: A nut cracker up in a tree, with stripes on its back.
altered_reasoning: Chipmunks crack nuts, are arboreal, and have distinctive stripes on their backs. Squirrels lack stripes, so they no longer fit the description, making chipmunk the logical answer.

original_riddle: What is so fragile that saying its name breaks it?
original_reasoning: The riddle is a classic wordplay where the act of answering the riddle is the act that destroys the answer.
altered_riddle: What is so fragile that saying its name breaks it, and it is the formal promise of a monk not to speak?
altered_reasoning: This variation tests whether the model can distinguish between the state of 'silence' and the formal commitment known as a 'vow'. While silence is the absence of sound, the 'vow of silence' is the religious promise itself. If the monk speaks the name of the vow, he has committed an act of speech, thereby breaking the promise. This specifically addresses the 'formal promise' constraint which 'silence' alone does not satisfy.

original_riddle: What has a neck and no head, two arms but no hands?
original_reasoning: A shirt has a collar (neck), no head, two sleeves (arms), but no hands.
altered_riddle: What has a neck and no head, two arms but no hands, but is not a piece of clothing?
altered_reasoning: The explicit negation 'not a piece of clothing' eliminates shirt. Trophies often have a stem (neck), no head, two handles (arms), and no hands, fitting the description.

original_riddle: White bird, featherless, flying out of paradise. Flying over sea and land. Dying in my hand.
original_reasoning: Snow is white, looks like it is flying as it falls from the clouds (paradise), covers both sea and land, and melts (dies) when it touches a warm hand.
altered_riddle: White bird, featherless, flying out of paradise. Flying over sea and land. It does not melt, yet it dies in my hand.
altered_reasoning: The phrase 'It does not melt' explicitly contradicts the primary characteristic of snow. A dandelion seed is white, featherless (it has pappus, not feathers), flies from the 'paradise' of a garden, travels over land and sea, and 'dies' (is crushed or dismantled) when caught in a hand.

original_riddle: The more of it there is, the less you see.
original_reasoning: Darkness obscures vision; as darkness increases, visibility decreases.
altered_riddle: The more of it there is, the less you see, and it accumulates on the ground.
altered_reasoning: Snow accumulates on the ground (physical substance), and heavy snowfall reduces visibility. Darkness does not physically accumulate on the ground.

original_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Under their emerald embraceEither in their life or in their death.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes trees as powerful, graded by size or age, beautiful and strong, and as the things under whose emerald canopy many living things exist and return to the earth in death.
altered_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Under their emerald embrace...Either in their life or in their death, they float on the sea.
altered_reasoning: The added phrase 'they float on the sea' changes the setting from land to water. Trees do not float on the sea, so they no longer fit. Seaweed can be described as living under an emerald embrace in the ocean, where many creatures depend on it and where dead sea plants also drift and support life.

original_riddle: I have a neck but no head. I have a body but no arm. I have a bottom but no leg.
original_reasoning: A bottle has a neck (the narrow part), a body (the main container), and a bottom (base), but lacks human anatomy like heads, arms, or legs.
altered_riddle: I have a neck but no head. I have a body but no arm. I have a bottom but no leg. I have strings but no voice.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'I have strings' rules out the bottle. A guitar possesses a neck, a body, and a bottom end. While guitar parts are named after anatomy (headstock/neck/body), in the context of the riddle's metaphors, it fits the description of having strings but no biological voice.

original_riddle: Walk on the living, they don’t even mumble. Walk on the dead, they mutter and grumble.
original_reasoning: Dry, fallen leaves (dead) crunch and rustle underfoot, creating a muttering sound, while living leaves on trees make no noise when brushed against.
altered_riddle: Walk on the living, they don’t even mumble. Walk on the dead, they mutter and grumble, yet they contain no chlorophyll.
altered_reasoning: The riddle explicitly denies chlorophyll, forcing rejection of the memorized botanical answer (leaves). Living grain in the field is silent, while processed, 'dead' cereal flakes crunch and mutter when walked on.

original_riddle: What goes up when the rain comes down?
original_reasoning: Umbrella opens upward during rain.
altered_riddle: What goes up when the rain comes down, but does not have a handle?
altered_reasoning: A rain gauge collects rain, causing the water level inside to rise (go up). It has no handle, whereas an umbrella does, so the answer is a rain gauge.

original_riddle: What gets bigger the more you take away from it?
original_reasoning: A hole is defined by the absence of material; removing more material from its location increases the size of the void.
altered_riddle: What gets bigger the more you take away from its denominator, rather than digging into the ground?
altered_reasoning: This variation explicitly addresses the 'hole' bias by mentioning 'digging into the ground' as a distractor, then pointing to the mathematical concept of a 'denominator.' In a positive fraction, if you 'take away' from (decrease) the denominator, the total value of the fraction increases.

original_riddle: Take off my skin, I won't cry, but you will.
original_reasoning: Peeling an onion releases irritant compounds that cause tears, while the onion itself does not cry.
altered_riddle: Take off my skin, I won't cry, but you will, and my skin is transparent.
altered_reasoning: This probes bias that skin is opaque. Windows have glass panes (skin) that are transparent. Removing glass won't make it cry, but you might cry if injured. Onion's skin isn't transparent, so window is correct.

original_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Under their emerald embrace… Either in their life or in their death.
original_reasoning: The 'emerald embrace' refers to the green canopy of leaves. Trees provide the oxygen and environment necessary for most life on land, and their dead wood provides habitat and nutrients.
altered_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Though they possess no roots nor trunks, they offer an emerald embrace… Either in their life or in their death.
altered_reasoning: The altered riddle explicitly states they have 'no roots nor trunks', which rules out trees. Coral polyps are animals ('creatures'), often appear emerald/green due to symbiotic algae, and their calcium carbonate skeletons (their 'death') create the reef structure that supports the majority of ocean biodiversity.

original_riddle: The more you take, the more you leave behind.
original_reasoning: When walking, each step taken leaves a physical footprint or impression behind. The act of taking steps inherently creates a trail.
altered_riddle: The more you take, the more you leave behind, even if you never move.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'even if you never move' disqualifies steps, which require physical movement. Photos can be taken from a stationary position, and each photo taken leaves a digital file or memory behind.

original_riddle: What has a single eye but cannot see?
original_reasoning: A sewing needle has a single eye, and despite having an eye, it cannot see.
altered_riddle: What has a single eye but cannot see in the ocean?
altered_reasoning: In this context, 'eye' refers to the calm center of a hurricane, which is a single eye. A hurricane cannot see, and the added setting 'in the ocean' naturally shifts the answer from a sewing needle to a weather system.

original_riddle: The moon is my father. The sea is my mother. I have a million brothers. I die when I reach land.
original_reasoning: Waves are generated by the moon's gravity (father) and the sea (mother). There are many waves (brothers). Waves break and disappear (die) when they hit the shore (land).
altered_riddle: The moon is my father. The sea is my mother. I have a million brothers. I fly when I reach land.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'die' to 'fly' points to a different phenomenon. While the bulk of a wave 'dies' on the shore, the droplets flung into the air become sea spray, satisfying the condition of 'flying' upon contact with land.

original_riddle: It's in your hand though you can not feel it. Only you and time can reveal it.
original_reasoning: Fate is said to be in one's hands (under one's control), is intangible (cannot be felt), and is revealed only through the passage of time and one's actions.
altered_riddle: It's in your hand though you can not feel it. It is not fate. Only you and time can reveal it, as the stone is cut.
altered_reasoning: Explicitly stating 'It is not fate' contradicts the memorized answer to test for bias. Adding 'as the stone is cut' introduces lapidary context. A rough gemstone is held in the hand, the finished facets are hidden inside (cannot be felt), and are revealed only by the holder cutting it over time.

original_riddle: When young, I am sweet in the sun. When middle-aged, I make you gay. When old, I am valued more than ever.
original_reasoning: Grapes, wine, and vintage wine.
altered_riddle: When young, I am sweet in the sun. When middle-aged, I make you gay. When old, I am a sour liquid used on a salad.
altered_reasoning: This is a bias probe. The riddle begins exactly like the famous wine riddle, leading the model to expect the answer 'wine'. However, the final sentence explicitly states that the 'old' version is a 'sour liquid used on a salad', which describes vinegar. While wine turns into vinegar if aged poorly, the prompt defines the final state as a common salad dressing, which invalidates 'wine' as the final valued product.

original_riddle: What occurs once in a minute, twice in a moment, and never in a thousand years?
original_reasoning: The letter M appears once in the word 'minute', twice in the word 'moment', and does not appear in the phrase 'thousand years'.
altered_riddle: What occurs once in a minute, twice in a miniature, and never in a thousand years?
altered_reasoning: The letter I appears once in the word 'minute', twice in the word 'miniature' (m-i-n-i-a-t-u-r-e), and does not appear in the phrase 'thousand years'. The original answer M does not work because M does not appear in 'miniature' at all.

original_riddle: If you have it, you want to share it. If you share it, you don't have it.
original_reasoning: A secret is defined by its exclusivity; once it is shared with others, it is no longer a secret.
altered_riddle: If you have it, you want to spend it. If you spend it, you don't have it.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'share' to 'spend' shifts the meaning from the transmission of information to the exchange of currency. You want to spend money to acquire things, and once spent, the money is gone from your possession.

original_riddle: A man leaves home, takes three left turns, and arrives back home facing two men wearing masks. Who are the masked men?
original_reasoning: The riddle relies on baseball terminology where 'home' is home plate. Running a square path (three left turns) returns a batter to home plate, where the catcher and umpire stand wearing protective masks.
altered_riddle: A man leaves home, takes three left turns, and arrives back home facing two men wearing masks and scrubs. Who are the masked men?
altered_reasoning: Adding 'and scrubs' introduces a medical constraint. Catchers and umpires do not wear scrubs, but doctors commonly wear masks and scrubs in a medical setting.

original_riddle: I run through hills. I veer around mountains. I leap over rivers. And crawl through the forests. Step out your door to find me.
original_reasoning: A road runs through hills, curves around mountains, crosses rivers by bridges, and passes through forests. It is also something you commonly find just outside your door.
altered_riddle: I run through hills. I veer around mountains. I leap over rivers. And crawl through the forests. Step out your door to hear me.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'find me' to 'hear me' shifts the riddle toward something perceived by sound rather than seen physically. Wind moves through hills, around mountains, over rivers, and through forests, and when you step outside you can hear it. A road is something you find or see, not something you hear in itself, so the original answer is invalidated.

original_riddle: I'm the source of all emotion, but I'm caged in a white prison.
original_reasoning: The heart is metaphorically the source of emotion, and it is encased in the ribcage, which appears white in skeletal diagrams.
altered_riddle: I'm the source of all locomotion, but I'm caged in a white prison.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'emotion' to 'locomotion' shifts the target organ. The brain controls movement (locomotion) and is encased in the skull, which is a white bone prison. The heart does not control locomotion.

original_riddle: I have legs but walk not, a strong back but work not. Two good arms but reach not. A seat but sit and tarry not.
original_reasoning: A chair has legs (that don't walk), a backrest (that doesn't perform labor), armrests (that don't reach), and a seat (upon which others sit, while the chair itself remains stationary).
altered_riddle: I have legs but walk not, a strong back but work not. Two good arms but reach not. A seat but sit and tarry not, and I am found in the gym, spun by sweating hands.
altered_reasoning: Swapping the context to a gym and adding 'spun' (ridden) eliminates a domestic chair. An exercise bike has legs (frame legs), a strong back (seat support), two good arms (handlebars), and a seat. It walks not (stays in place or rolls on wheels), works not (the person works; the bike functions but does not labor), reaches not, and the bike itself does not sit.

original_riddle: Forward I’m heavy, but backwards I’m not.
original_reasoning: The word 'ton' is a unit of heavy weight, and when spelled backwards, it becomes the word 'not'.
altered_riddle: Forward I'm heavy with gloom, but backwards I'm a mood.
altered_reasoning: The word 'heavy' is shifted from a physical weight to an emotional or thematic weight. 'Doom' is a heavy fate or state of gloom. When reversed, 'doom' spells 'mood'. The original answer 'ton' does not work because it is not 'mood' backwards, nor does it fit the context of 'gloom'.

original_riddle: High born, my touch is gentle. Purest white is my lace. Silence is my kingdom. Green is the color of my death.
original_reasoning: Snow is formed high in the atmosphere, falls gently, is white and can appear lacy, creates silence when it falls, and when it melts, it reveals green grass or plants.
altered_riddle: High born, my touch is gentle. Purest white is my lace. Silence is my kingdom. Green is the color of my death, and I never fall from the sky.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'and I never fall from the sky' breaks snow, which falls from the sky. Clouds are high born, touch gently as mist, are white and lacy, have silent skies, and their death (rain) brings green to earth. Thus, green is the color of their death.

original_riddle: I make you weak at the worst of all times. I keep you safe, I keep you fine. I make your hands sweat. And your heart grow cold. I visit the weak, but seldom the bold.
original_reasoning: Fear causes physical symptoms like sweating and weakness, acts as a survival mechanism to keep one safe, and is typically associated with a lack of courage (the bold).
altered_riddle: I make you weak at the worst of all times. I keep you safe, I keep you fine. I make your hands sweat. And your heart grow cold. I visit the bold and the weak alike, for I am the instinct of survival.
altered_reasoning: The alteration explicitly defines the entity as the 'instinct of survival' and states it visits both the bold and the weak. This moves the answer from the emotion of 'fear' to the biological mechanism (fight-or-flight response) that governs the physical reactions described (sweating, heart rate changes, muscle weakness/tension) regardless of the person's courage.

original_riddle: What can bring back the dead. Make us cry, make us laugh, make us young. Born in an instant yet lasts a life time?
original_reasoning: Memories recall people who died, evoke strong emotions, restore feelings of youth through nostalgia, are formed quickly, and persist forever.
altered_riddle: What can bring back the dead. Make us cry, make us laugh, make us young. Born in an instant yet lasts a life time, and is written in ink?
altered_reasoning: Adding 'is written in ink' contradicts the abstract nature of memory. A letter records words from potentially deceased writers, evokes emotion, preserves past youth, is written quickly, lasts a lifetime, and is physically written in ink.

original_riddle: I look at you, you look at me, I raise my right, you raise your left.
original_reasoning: A mirror reflects a person's image. When you face a mirror and raise your right hand, the reflection appears to raise its left, creating the left-right reversal described.
altered_riddle: I look at you, you look at me, I raise my right, you raise your left, and I stand across from you on the stage.
altered_reasoning: The added phrase places the scene in a real shared space rather than a reflection. If someone stands across from you and faces you, then when they raise their right hand, it appears on your left side and naturally corresponds to your raising your left to mirror them. A mirror no longer fits because it does not stand across from you on a stage as another person does.

original_riddle: I open wide and tight I shut, Sharp am I and paper-cut fingers too, so do take care, I'm good and bad, so best beware.
original_reasoning: Scissors open and close, are sharp, cut paper/fingers, and are useful yet dangerous.
altered_riddle: I open wide and tight I shut, Sharp am I and pierce fingers too, so do take care, I'm good and bad, so best beware. I bind, I do not cut.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'paper-cut' to 'pierce' and adding 'I bind, I do not cut' shifts the function. A stapler opens and shuts, has sharp metal points that pierce, can hurt fingers, and binds paper rather than cutting it. 'Good' for binding, 'bad' for injury.

original_riddle: What force and strength cannot get through. I, with a gentle touch, can do. Many in the street would stand. Were I not a friend at hand.
original_reasoning: A key opens what force cannot break. A key is held in the hand and prevents people from being stuck outside in the street.
altered_riddle: What force and strength cannot undo. I, with a gentle touch, can do. Many in the prison would stand. Were I not a friend at hand.
altered_reasoning: Swapping 'get through' to 'undo' and 'street' to 'prison' changes the context to legal imprisonment. A signature on a pardon or writ undoes a sentence with the gentle touch of a pen, whereas force cannot undo justice. A key cannot legally free a prisoner.

original_riddle: My life is often a volume of grief, your help is needed to turn a new leaf. Stiff is my spine and my body is pale. But I'm always ready to tell a tale.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes a book using parts like the spine and pages.
altered_riddle: My life is often a volume of grief, your help is needed to turn a new leaf. Stiff is my spine and my body is pale, and I am encased in rock to tell a tale.
altered_reasoning: The addition of 'encased in rock' moves the context from a library to geology. In this context, 'spine' refers to the fossilized vertebrae of a creature, 'pale' refers to the mineralized bone, and 'turn a new leaf' refers to splitting or turning over the 'leaves' (strata/layers) of sedimentary rock to reveal the find.

original_riddle: When you stop and look, you can always see me. If you try to touch, you cannot feel me. I cannot move, but as you near me, I will move away from you.
original_reasoning: The horizon is always visible when you look, intangible to touch, stationary as a geographical line, but appears to recede as you approach due to the curvature of the Earth and perspective.
altered_riddle: When you stop and look, you can always see me. If you try to touch, you cannot feel me. I cannot move, but as you near me, I will move away from you. I vanish after sunset.
altered_reasoning: The added clause 'I vanish after sunset' contradicts the original answer 'horizon', which remains present though not visible in darkness. Rainbows are optical phenomena that disappear after sunset as they require sunlight. They are visible, intangible, stationary in appearance, and recede upon approach due to changing angles. This alteration probes the bias to default to 'horizon' without considering the new constraint.

original_riddle: I am an odd number. Take away a letter and I become even. What number am I?
original_reasoning: The word 'seven' is an odd number; removing the letter 's' leaves the word 'even'.
altered_riddle: I am an odd number. Take away a digit and I become even. What number am I?
altered_reasoning: The change from 'letter' to 'digit' shifts the logic from spelling to mathematics. 'Seven' has no digits to remove. '21' is an odd number; if you remove the digit '1', the remaining digit is '2', which is an even number.

original_riddle: Alive without breath, As cold as death, Clad in mail never clinking, Never thirsty, ever drinking
original_reasoning: Fish are alive but use gills instead of lungs (without breath), are cold-blooded (cold as death), have scales (mail), and live in water (ever drinking).
altered_riddle: Sails without breath, As cold as death, Clad in mail never clinking, Never thirsty, ever drinking
altered_reasoning: Changing 'Alive' to 'Sails' shifts the context from biological to mechanical. Ships sail, have no breath, are cold (metal), can be armored (mail), and float on water (drink). Fish swim, not sail.

original_riddle: What runs around all day. Then lies under the bed. With its tongue hanging out?
original_reasoning: A shoe 'runs around all day' because people wear it while walking and running. Later it may be left under the bed, and a shoe has a 'tongue' that can stick out.
altered_riddle: What runs around all day, then lies under the bed, with its tongue hanging out, and it isn't clothing?
altered_reasoning: Many solvers jump to 'shoe' from memory, but the added clause explicitly blocks that because a shoe is an item of clothing or apparel. A dog still fits all the remaining clues: it can run around all day, lie under the bed, and have its tongue hanging out. This alteration is designed to probe memorized-answer bias by directly excluding the classic answer.

original_riddle: I cost no money to use, or conscious effort to take part of. And as far as you can see, there is nothing to me. But without me, you are dead.
original_reasoning: Air is the standard answer for an invisible, free, essential substance.
altered_riddle: I cost no money to use, or conscious effort to take part of. And as far as you can see, there is nothing to me. But without me, you are in the dark.
altered_reasoning: Changing the consequence 'you are dead' to 'you are in the dark' changes the answer from a substance required for biological respiration to a phenomenon required for vision. Light is free, hits our eyes without conscious effort, and is invisible (transparent) until it strikes an object. Without it, one is in the dark.

original_riddle: My life can be measured in hours. I serve by being devoured. Thin, I am quick. Fat, I am slow. Wind is my foe.
original_reasoning: A candle burns down (is devoured) to produce light, with thin candles burning quickly and thick candles burning slowly; wind extinguishes the flame.
altered_riddle: My life can be measured in hours. I serve by being devoured. Thin, I am quick. Fat, I am slow. Wind is my foe. I am not made of wax.
altered_reasoning: Explicitly stating 'I am not made of wax' breaks the memorized 'candle' answer. Wood burns (is devoured) to provide heat; thin kindling burns quickly while thick logs burn slowly; wind endangers fires; and the lifespan is measured in burn hours.

original_riddle: What is it that was given to you, belongs only to you, and yet your friends use it more than you do?
original_reasoning: A person's name is given by their parents and belongs to their identity, but other people (friends, family, etc.) are the ones who use the name to call or refer to them, while the owner rarely says it themselves.
altered_riddle: What is it that was given to you, belongs only to you, and yet your friends take it more than you do?
altered_reasoning: The change to the word 'take' creates a meaning shift. While 'use' refers to verbal utility (a name), 'take' in the context of personal identity refers to capturing a photograph. A picture of you (your likeness) belongs to you, but your friends are the ones who 'take' your picture far more often than you take selfies or your own portraits.

original_riddle: My children are near and far. No matter that I know where they are. The gift I give them make their day. But if I were gone they would wander away.
original_reasoning: The sun's children can be seen as planets or light rays that are near and far. The sun gives the gift of light and warmth that makes the day. If the sun were gone, the planets would wander away from their orbits.
altered_riddle: My children are near and far. No matter that I know where they are. The gift I give them make their day. But if I were gone they would wander away, and I am not a star.
altered_reasoning: The added constraint 'and I am not a star' directly contradicts the sun, which is a star. A parent has children who are near and far, knows where they are, gives gifts that make their day, and if the parent is gone, the children might wander away. This probes the bias of assuming the answer is 'sun' without considering alternatives.

original_riddle: Stealthy as a shadow in the dead of night, cunning but affectionate if given a bite. Never owned but often loved. At my sport considered cruel, but that's because you never know me at all.
original_reasoning: Cats are known for being stealthy, nocturnal, cunning, and having an independent nature ('never owned'). Their 'sport' (hunting rodents/birds) is often viewed as cruel by humans.
altered_riddle: Stealthy as a shadow in the dead of night, cunning but affectionate if given a bite. I am a loyal pet owned by many. At my sport considered cruel, but that's because you never know me at all.
altered_reasoning: The original riddle relies on the 'never owned' trait to point toward the independent nature of cats. By explicitly stating 'I am a loyal pet owned by many', the riddle contradicts the 'independent/unownable' trope of the cat and points toward the dog. Dogs are also stealthy hunters and can be considered 'cruel' during their 'sport' (hunting/retrieving), but are famously loyal pets.

original_riddle: What goes in the water black and comes out red?
original_reasoning: Lobsters are typically dark or blackish when alive in the water, but turn bright red when cooked and removed from the water.
altered_riddle: What goes in the water black and comes out red, as a signal?
altered_reasoning: The phrase 'as a signal' shifts the meaning of 'comes out red' from physical color to light emission. A sea flare has a black casing and emits a red light when activated in water, whereas a lobster does not emit light.

original_riddle: Flat as a leaf, round as a ring. Has two eyes, can't see a thing.
original_reasoning: Buttons are typically flat and round, with two or four holes (eyes) for thread. As inanimate objects, they cannot see.
altered_riddle: Flat as a leaf, round as a ring. Has one eye, can't see a thing.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'two eyes' to 'one eye' breaks the button solution (which typically requires at least two holes to be functional or recognized as such in the riddle context). A keyhole is often set in a flat, round escutcheon (plate), has a single opening (eye), and cannot see.

original_riddle: You hear it speak, for it has a hard tongue. But it cannot breathe, for it has not a lung.
original_reasoning: A bell produces sound (speaks) via a metal clapper inside it (its hard tongue). Being inanimate metal, it does not breathe or possess lungs.
altered_riddle: You hear it speak at your waist, for it has a hard tongue. But it cannot breathe, for it has not a lung.
altered_reasoning: The context is shifted from a tower or high place to one's waist. A belt buckle possesses a metal prong (hard tongue) that inserts into holes, clicking or snapping into place (speaking). It has no lung. The original answer 'bell' does not fit the context of being worn at the waist.

original_riddle: What starts with a 'T', ends with a 'T', and has T in it?
original_reasoning: The riddle relies on wordplay and a homophone: the word 'teapot' begins and ends with the letter 't', and the physical object holds 'tea' (T).
altered_riddle: What starts with a 'T', ends with a 'T', has a 'T' in it, and automatically regulates the temperature of a room?
altered_reasoning: The addition of regulating room temperature points out a new specific item. The word 'thermostat' literally starts with a 'T', ends with a 'T', and has an internal 't' (thermosTat), making it flawlessly fit both the spelling constraints and the new functional constraint.

original_riddle: I have split the one into five. I am the circle that few will spy. I am the path that breaks and gives. I am the bow no man may bend.
original_reasoning: A rainbow splits white light into colors (often simplified to five), appears as a circular arc that is rarely seen as a full circle, the light path breaks through rain and gives way to colors, and it is shaped like a bow that cannot be physically bent.
altered_riddle: I have split the one into five. I am the circle of glass that few will spy. I am the path that breaks and gives. I am the bow no man may bend.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'of glass' introduces a material constraint that rules out rainbow, as rainbows are not made of glass. A stained glass window, particularly a circular rose window, splits white light into colored light (five colors), is a circular shape often located in high places and rarely closely observed, the light path through the glass breaks and gives color, and it is arched like a bow and cannot be bent because it is fixed glass.

original_riddle: What kind of nut is empty at the center and has no shell?
original_reasoning: A doughnut contains the word 'nut', is typically shaped with a hole (empty center), and unlike actual nuts, it has no shell.
altered_riddle: What kind of nut is empty at the center, has no shell, and is made of steel?
altered_reasoning: The riddle explicitly states the object is 'made of steel', which directly contradicts the composition of a doughnut (flour, sugar, etc.), forcing the answer to be the hardware nut.

original_riddle: What loses its head in the morning and gets it back at night?
original_reasoning: When you sleep at night, your head rests on the pillow (it has a head). When you wake up in the morning, you lift your head off (it loses its head).
altered_riddle: What loses its head in the morning and gets it back at night, and is made of iron?
altered_reasoning: The constraint 'made of iron' rules out pillow. A helmet worn by a night shift worker loses its 'head' (the worker's head inside it) when work ends in the morning, and gets it back when work starts at night.

original_riddle: What always runs but never walks, often murmurs, never talks, has a bed but never sleeps, has a mouth but never eats?
original_reasoning: A river runs (flows), has a murmur (sound of water), has a riverbed, and has a river mouth, fitting all the metaphorical descriptions.
altered_riddle: What always runs but never walks, often murmurs, never talks, has a bed but never sleeps, has a font but never eats?
altered_reasoning: Changing 'mouth' to 'font' creates a new solution. A printing press runs (operates), murmurs (mechanical noise), has a 'bed' (the flat surface for the type), and utilizes a 'font' (set of type). It does not eat.

original_riddle: I'm sometimes white and always wrong. I can break a heart and hurt the strong. I can build love or tear it down. I can make a smile or bring a frown.
original_reasoning: A 'white lie' is a minor untruth; lies are morally wrong; they can break hearts and hurt anyone; they can create false intimacy or destroy trust; they can make people happy (white lies) or sad.
altered_riddle: I'm sometimes white and never wrong. I can break a heart and hurt the strong. I can build love or tear it down. I can make a smile or bring a frown.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'always wrong' to 'never wrong' (i.e., always right) reverses the logical requirements. Truth is often associated with white/purity; it is definitionally never wrong; hard truths can break hearts and hurt even strong people; honesty builds genuine love or destroys false relationships; good news brings smiles, bad news brings frowns.

original_riddle: I'm light as a feather, yet the strongest man can't hold me for more than 5 minutes. What am I?
original_reasoning: Breath is metaphorically weightless, and biologically, a human cannot hold their breath for more than a few minutes without passing out, regardless of physical strength.
altered_riddle: I am literally a bird's plumage. I'm light as a feather, yet the strongest man can't hold me for more than 5 minutes while standing squarely within a raging tornado. What am I?
altered_reasoning: This alteration explicitly tells the reader exactly what the object is ('literally a bird's plumage') to override the metaphorical trick. A model biased toward outputting 'breath' due to recognizing the riddle structure will ignore the literal definition provided.

original_riddle: Metal or bone I may be, many teeth I have and always bared. Yet my bite harms no one. And ladies delight in my touch.
original_reasoning: Combs can be made of metal or bone, have many teeth that are always exposed, their teeth do not bite or harm, and ladies enjoy using combs on their hair.
altered_riddle: Metal or bone I may be, many teeth I have and always bared. Yet my bite harms no one. And ladies delight in my touch, though not for hair.
altered_reasoning: The added phrase 'though not for hair' explicitly rules out combs, which are primarily used for hair. Fans can be made of metal or bone, have many ribs (teeth) that are always bared when open, their breeze (bite) is harmless, and ladies delight in the cooling touch of a fan. This alteration tests the bias that 'teeth' and 'ladies' automatically imply a comb.

original_riddle: I am the beginning of the end, and the end of time and space. I am essential to creation, and I surround every place. What am I?
original_reasoning: The riddle uses wordplay; the letter 'e' is the first letter of the word 'end' and the last letter of the words 'time', 'space', 'creation', and 'place'.
altered_riddle: I am the beginning of the end, and the end of time and space. I am essential to creation, and I surround every place, but I am not a letter. What am I?
altered_reasoning: By explicitly stating 'I am not a letter', the original answer (the letter E) is logically disqualified. This forces the solver to interpret the riddle metaphorically. The universe encompasses the end of time/space (the horizon), is essential for creation to exist, and by definition surrounds every place.

original_riddle: What building has the most stories?
original_reasoning: The riddle relies on the double meaning of 'stories'. A library contains the most narrative stories (books), not necessarily the most floor levels.
altered_riddle: What building has the most stories, specifically floor levels?
altered_reasoning: Explicitly defining 'stories' as 'floor levels' counters the bias that 'stories' refers to books. Libraries do not typically have the most floors, whereas skyscrapers are designed to maximize floor levels.

original_riddle: I can be moved. I can be rolled. But nothing will I hold. I'm red and I'm blue, and I can be other colors too. Having no head, though similar in shape. I have no eyes - yet move all over the place.
original_reasoning: A ball is a round object that rolls, can be moved, comes in colors, and has no head or eyes.
altered_riddle: I can be moved. I can be rolled. But nothing will I hold. I'm red and I'm blue, and I can be other colors too. Having no head, though similar in shape. I am swallowed.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'I am swallowed' changes the context to medicine. Pills are often round (rolled), come in colors (red/blue), hold nothing (solid), and are swallowed, distinguishing them from a play ball.

original_riddle: Forward I'm heavy, but backwards I'm not.
original_reasoning: The word 'ton' is a heavy unit of weight (2000 pounds). Spelled backwards, 'ton' becomes 'not', which is simply a negation and not a weight.
altered_riddle: Forward I'm heavy, but backwards I'm not. I have four letters, not three, and I often accompany 'gloom'.
altered_reasoning: The alteration explicitly provides constraints (four letters, accompanies 'gloom') that contradict the memorized answer 'ton' (three letters, does not accompany 'gloom'). 'Doom' is a heavy burden or fate; backwards it spells 'mood', which is not inherently heavy. This tests whether the model ignores explicit text favoring a memorized pattern.

original_riddle: Thirty white horses on a red hill, First they champ, Then they stamp, Then they stand still.
original_reasoning: The thirty white horses represent human teeth. The red hill represents the gums and tongue. Champing and stamping refer to the act of biting and chewing food, after which the mouth rests.
altered_riddle: Thirty white horses on a rotating hill, First they rise, Then they fall, Then they stand still.
altered_reasoning: Replacing 'red' with 'rotating' and the chewing actions with the constrained movements 'rise' and 'fall' adds mechanical, spatial constraints. A carousel features white horses that physically rise and fall on mechanical poles as the ride rotates, and then stand still when the ride finishes. Teeth vibrate but do not independently rise and fall on a continuously rotating surface.

original_riddle: When there is fire in me then I am still cold. When I own your true love’s face then you will not see me. To all things I give no more than I am given. In time I may have all things, and yet I can keep nothing.
original_reasoning: A mirror reflects fire but is not hot, reflects a face making the mirror itself 'invisible' as you see the reflection, gives back only what it receives, and reflects everything over time but retains nothing.
altered_riddle: When there is fire in me then I am still cold. When I own your true love’s face then you will not see me. To all things I give no more than I am given. In time I may have all things, and yet I can keep nothing. I am woven from fibers.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'woven from fibers' (e.g., photographic paper) rules out a mirror (typically glass/metal). A photograph depicts fire but is not hot, contains a loved one's face making the physical photo secondary, captures only what was given to it, and over time can depict all things but keeps nothing tangible. The original answer fails the new material constraint.

original_riddle: Late afternoons I often bathe. I'll soak in water piping hot. My essence goes through. My see through clothes. Used up am I - I've gone to pot.
original_reasoning: A teabag is soaked in hot water to release tea essence, has a paper or muslin wrapper (clothes), and is discarded or composted (gone to pot) after use.
altered_riddle: Late afternoons I often bathe. I'll soak in water piping hot. My essence goes through. I lack clothes. Used up am I - I've gone to pot.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'My see through clothes' to 'I lack clothes' directly contradicts the definition of a teabag, which has a wrapper. This probes the model's bias towards the memorized answer 'teabag'. Loose leaf tea fits the bathing and essence extraction without a container.

original_riddle: What goes around the world and stays in a corner?
original_reasoning: A stamp is placed in the corner of an envelope and travels around the world via postal services.
altered_riddle: What goes around the world and stays in a corner, and *holds your clothes*?
altered_reasoning: Adding the constraint 'holds your clothes' disqualifies a stamp. A suitcase travels around the world, is often stored in a corner of a room or luggage rack, and contains clothing.

original_riddle: Turns us on our backs, and open up our stomachs. You will be the wisest of men though at start a lummox.
original_reasoning: Books are turned onto their backs (laid flat with the spine facing down), opened up (their pages/stomachs are accessed), and reading them imparts wisdom to the reader who starts out ignorant (a lummox).
altered_riddle: Turns us on our backs, and open up our stomachs. You will be the fullest of men though at start a lummox.
altered_reasoning: The meaning of 'wisest' shifts to 'fullest,' changing the context from knowledge to satiation. Lunchboxes are turned on their backs (laid flat), opened up (stomach/lid unlatched), and contain food that fills a hungry person (a lummox implies emptiness/need). Books do not alleviate hunger.

original_riddle: Three lives have I. Gentle enough to soothe the skin. Light enough to caress the sky. Hard enough to crack rocks.
original_reasoning: Water exists in three states (lives): as a soft liquid that soothes skin, as a vapor or cloud that sits in the sky, and as solid ice that expands to crack rocks.
altered_riddle: Once part of a bird was I. Gentle enough to soothe the skin. Light enough to caress the sky. Sharp enough to write a letter with ink.
altered_reasoning: The context shifts from states of matter to an animal by-product. Water does not come from birds or write with ink. Feathers are notoriously soft (soothing to touch), allow birds to fly (caress the sky), and historically formed quills that are sharp enough to dip in ink and write.

original_riddle: I have one eye. See near and far. I hold the moments you treasure and the things that make you weep.
original_reasoning: A camera has a single lens (eye), can focus on subjects near or far, and stores photographs of important or emotional moments.
altered_riddle: I have one eye. See near and far. I hold the moments you treasure and the things that make you weep, and I produce tears.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'produce tears' eliminates the camera, which is mechanical. A human eye sees near and far, witnesses moments, and physically cries (produces tears) when emotional.

original_riddle: A dragons tooth in a mortals hand, I kill, I maim, I divide the land.
original_reasoning: The 'dragon's tooth' refers to the steel blade, and swords are weapons used to kill, maim, and conquer territory (divide the land).
altered_riddle: A dragons tooth in a mortals hand, I kill, I maim, I divide the land, and I am made of ivory.
altered_reasoning: This probes the bias that 'dragon's tooth' is a metaphor for a steel sword. By explicitly stating the material is 'ivory', the answer becomes literal. A literal dragon's tooth is made of ivory/bone, not steel, and if wielded as a weapon, it satisfies the physical description.

original_riddle: He has married many women but has never married.
original_reasoning: A priest conducts wedding ceremonies for many couples (marrying the women to their partners) but takes vows of celibacy or simply never enters into matrimony himself.
altered_riddle: He has married many women but has never married, and he does not believe in God.
altered_reasoning: This explicitly contradicts the common bias that wedding officiants are religious figures like priests. The altered riddle states the subject is an atheist (non-religious), pointing to a secular officiant such as a judge who performs civil ceremonies but has never personally married.

original_riddle: My first is in wield, sever bones and marrow. My second is in blade, forged in cold steel. My third is an arbalest, and also in arrows. My fourth is in power, plunged through a shield. My fifth is in honor, and also in vows. My last will put an end to it all.
original_reasoning: This is an acrostic spelling riddle. The first letter is 'W' (in wield), the second is 'E' (in blade/steel), the third is 'A' (arbalest/arrows), the fourth is 'P' (power/plunged), the fifth is 'O' (honor/vows), and the last is 'N' (which puts an 'end' to 'weapon' or comes from 'aN eNd'). Together, they spell 'weapon'.
altered_riddle: My first is in wield, sever bones and marrow. My second is in blade, forged in cold steel. My third is an arbalest, and also in arrows. My fourth is in power, plunged through a shield. My fifth is in honor, and also in vows. My last will put an end to it all. While these lines spell a tool of destruction, what part of speech is the resulting word?
altered_reasoning: The letters generated by the clues still formulate the word 'weapon'. However, a sentence is added at the end constraining the answer to grammatical classification. Because 'weapon' is a person, place, or thing, the correct answer to the riddle is 'a noun'.

original_riddle: There is an ancient invention. Still used in some parts of the world today. That allows people to see through walls.
original_reasoning: Windows are transparent openings installed in walls that allow light and vision to pass through while keeping the wall structure intact.
altered_riddle: There is an ancient invention. Still used in some parts of the world today. That allows people to see their reflection in walls.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'see through' to 'see their reflection in' shifts the function from transparency to reflection. Mirrors are ancient inventions often mounted on walls that show a reflection rather than what is behind the wall.

original_riddle: My voice is tender, my waist is slender and I'm often invited to play. Yet wherever I go, I must take my bow or else I have nothing to say.
original_reasoning: A violin has a 'waist' (the C-bouts), a tender 'voice' (sound), and is played with a bow. Without the bow, it cannot produce sound.
altered_riddle: My voice is tender, my waist is slender and I'm often invited to play. Yet wherever I go, I must take my bow or else I have nothing to say, **for I am the one who holds the instrument**.
altered_reasoning: The added clause 'for I am the one who holds the instrument' clarifies that the speaker is a person, not the object. While a violinist creates the tender voice and has a slender waist, they are distinct from the violin because they are the agent 'holding' it. The violin itself cannot hold an instrument. This probes the bias of assuming the riddle refers to the object rather than the operator.

original_riddle: A word I know, six letters it contains. Subtract just one and twelve remains.
original_reasoning: The word 'dozens' has six letters. Removing the final letter 's' (subtracting one letter) leaves 'dozen', which means twelve.
altered_riddle: A word I know, four letters it contains. Add just one and twelve remains.
altered_reasoning: The operation changes from subtraction to addition, and the length changes from six to four. The word 'doze' has four letters; adding the letter 'n' creates 'dozen', which means twelve.

original_riddle: Always old, sometimes new. Never sad, sometimes blue. Never empty, sometimes full. Never pushes, always pulls.
original_reasoning: The riddle uses wordplay about lunar phases and properties: it is ancient but has a 'new moon' phase; inanimate but can be a 'blue moon'; retains its physical mass (never empty) but has a 'full moon' phase; and exerts an attractive gravitational pull on Earth's tides without repelling.
altered_riddle: Often old, sometimes new. Never sad, sometimes blue. Sometimes empty, sometimes full. By its long handle, it is what you pull.
altered_reasoning: The context is completely swapped from a cosmic entity to a children's toy. Softening the absolutes to 'Often old' and 'Sometimes empty' perfectly describes a literal wagon, which can be retro or new, painted blue, empty or loaded with items, and is functionally designed to be pulled by its distinct long handle.

original_riddle: At the sound of me, one may dream or stamp their feet, At the sound of me, one may laugh or sometimes weep.
original_reasoning: Music is heard (sound) and evokes various emotional and physical reactions like dreaming, dancing (stamp feet), laughing, or crying.
altered_riddle: At the sound of me, one may dream or stamp their feet, At the sound of me, one may laugh or sometimes weep, and I measure the hours.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'measure the hours' eliminates music. A clock makes sound (tick/tock/alarm), can induce dreaming (sleep time), stamping (waiting), or emotional reactions to time passing, and its primary function is measuring hours.

original_riddle: I have a face, yet no senses. But I don't really care, because time is of the essence.
original_reasoning: Clocks have a 'face' (the dial) and sometimes 'hands' but no biological senses. The phrase 'time is of the essence' confirms the object is related to timekeeping.
altered_riddle: I have a face, yet no senses. But I don't really care, because I am worn to hide.
altered_reasoning: Changing the final clause from 'time is of the essence' to 'I am worn to hide' completely changes the object's function. A mask has a face, no senses, and is worn to conceal identity, whereas a clock is not worn for hiding.

original_riddle: Almost everyone needs it, asks for it, gives it. But almost nobody takes it.
original_reasoning: The word 'takes' is used in the sense of 'accepts or follows.' People need advice, ask for advice, give advice, but often fail to take (heed) the advice they are given.
altered_riddle: Almost everyone needs it, asks for it, gives it. But almost nobody takes it, and it's prescribed by doctors.
altered_reasoning: Adding the constraint 'prescribed by doctors' eliminates abstract advice. Medicine fits: patients need it, ask for prescriptions, doctors/pharmacists give it, but studies show almost nobody takes their medicine exactly as prescribed (medication non-adherence is extremely common).

original_riddle: What kind of nut is empty at the center and has no shell.
original_reasoning: The riddle relies on wordplay. A doughnut is colloquially called a 'nut', has a hole in the center (empty), and lacks a hard outer shell.
altered_riddle: What kind of nut is empty at the center and has no shell, and is not edible.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'not edible' directly contradicts the food-based doughnut answer. A mechanical nut fits the physical description (hole, no shell) but is not food.

original_riddle: I saw a man in white, he looked quite a sight. He was not old, but he stood in the cold. And when he felt the sun, he started to run. Who could he be? Please answer me.
original_reasoning: A snowman fits the description of being white, standing in the cold, and melting (running water) in the sun.
altered_riddle: I saw a man in white, he looked quite a sight. He was not old, but he stood in the cold. And when he felt the sun, he started to slide.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'run' to 'slide' shifts the meaning from melting (liquid running) to a physical activity. A skier wears white, is in the cold, and slides on snow when the sun is out, whereas a snowman does not slide.

original_riddle: A skin have I, more eyes than one. I can be very nice when I am done.
original_reasoning: A potato has a skin (peel), multiple eyes (the buds that grow), and is nice to eat when cooked (done).
altered_riddle: A skin have I, more eyes than one. I can be very nice when I am done, and children cuddle me when they sleep.
altered_reasoning: The context is swapped from the culinary domain to the toy domain. The potato does not fit the description of being cuddled by children. A teddy bear has a skin (fur or fabric outer layer), more than one eye (typically two glass or plastic eyes), is 'nice' when done (finished being manufactured/sewn), and is specifically made to be cuddled by children.

original_riddle: This thing all things devours: Birds, beasts, trees, flowers; Gnaws iron, bites steel; Grinds hard stones to meal; Slays king, ruins town, And beats high mountains down.
original_reasoning: Time erodes and ends everything eventually. It consumes life, corrodes metal, weathers stone, ends kingdoms, and erodes mountains through natural processes.
altered_riddle: This thing all things devours: Birds, beasts, trees, flowers; Gnaws iron, bites steel; Grinds hard stones to meal; Slays king, ruins town, And beats high mountains down, in the sea.
altered_reasoning: Placing the action 'in the sea' shifts the context to a physical domain where Time is not the primary agent. Water devours via drowning and erosion, rusts iron, grinds stones via sediment, and shapes mountains via coastal erosion.

original_riddle: Tall in the morning, short at noon, gone at night. But I'll be back soon.
original_reasoning: A shadow's length varies with the sun's angle: tall in the morning, short at noon.
altered_riddle: Tall in the morning, short at noon, gone at night. But I'll be back soon, served with syrup.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'served with syrup' constrains the context to breakfast food. A stack of pancakes is tall when served in the morning, becomes short as it is eaten, and is gone by the end of the meal. While 'shadow' still fits the 'tall/short' adjectives, it fails the 'served with syrup' constraint.

original_riddle: My teeth are sharp, my back is straight, to cut things up it is my fate.
original_reasoning: A hand saw has sharp teeth along its cutting edge, a straight back (the spine opposite the teeth), and is designed specifically for cutting wood and other materials.
altered_riddle: My teeth are sharp, my back is straight, to cut things up it is my fate, as I glide through the ocean.
altered_reasoning: The context shifts from a workshop to the ocean. A sawfish is a marine creature with a long, straight rostrum (back) lined with sharp transverse teeth-like structures used to slash at prey, distinguishing it from a carpentry tool.

original_riddle: What is that over the head and under the hat?
original_reasoning: Hair grows on the surface of the head (over the head) and is covered when a hat is worn (under the hat).
altered_riddle: What is that around the head and under the hat?
altered_reasoning: Hair grows on top of the head, not around it. A headband wraps around the head and fits beneath a hat.

original_riddle: I am always hungry, I must always be fed. The finger I lick will soon turn red.
original_reasoning: Fire consumes fuel ('hungry/fed') and burns whatever it touches, turning skin red and causing pain.
altered_riddle: I am always hungry, I must always be fed. The finger I lick will soon turn red. I have nine lives.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'I have nine lives' forces the answer to be a cat. While cats are known for being hungry and licking, their rough tongues act like sandpaper, abrading the skin and turning it red if they lick repeatedly. Fire does not have lives.

original_riddle: I am the hole in the night, the ever watchful eye. I return in a cycle, to enlighten the sky.
original_reasoning: The moon appears as a circle (hole/disc) in the night sky, watches over Earth like an eye, goes through phases in a cyclical return, and reflects light to brighten the night.
altered_riddle: I am the hole in the night, the ever watchful ear. I return in a cycle, to listen to the sky.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'eye' to 'ear' and 'enlighten' to 'listen' shifts the sensory modality from visual to auditory. The moon does not listen. A radio telescope is a dish-shaped instrument (a concave hole) set against the night sky that rotates in cycles to listen to radio waves from space.

original_riddle: A warrior amongst the flowers, he bears a thrusting sword. He uses it whenever he must, to defend his golden hoard.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes a bee. 'Warrior amongst flowers' refers to its habitat and defensive nature. 'Thrusting sword' is its stinger. 'Golden hoard' is the honey it protects.
altered_riddle: A warrior amongst the waves, he bears a thrusting sword. He uses it whenever he must, to defend his golden hoard.
altered_reasoning: Swapping 'flowers' to 'waves' changes the setting from garden to sea. Bees inhabit flowers; pirates inhabit the sea. A pirate's cutlass is the sword, and buried treasure is the golden hoard.

original_riddle: I am two-faced but bear only one. I have no legs but travel widely. Men spill much blood over me. Kings leave their imprint on me. I have greatest power when given away, yet lust for me keeps me locked away.
original_reasoning: Coins are 'two-faced' (heads and tails) but usually bear a face on only one side. They travel through circulation. Blood is spilled over money. Kings are stamped on them. Money has power when spent, but greed (lust) keeps it hoarded.
altered_riddle: I am two-faced but bear only one. I have no legs but travel widely. Men spill much blood over me. Kings leave their imprint on me. I have greatest power when played, yet lust for me keeps me locked away.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'given away' to 'played' shifts the context from currency to gaming. Playing cards are 'two-faced' (front and back). They bear only one face (the King/Queen/Jack). They 'travel' in a shuffle. Blood is spilled over gambling. Kings are depicted on the face cards. They have power when played in a game, but cheating (lust for winning) might keep a card hidden or 'locked away' up a sleeve.

original_riddle: I'm light as a feather, yet the strongest man can't hold me for more than 5 minutes.
original_reasoning: Even a strong man can only hold his breath for a limited time before he must exhale; it is impossible to hold one's breath indefinitely.
altered_riddle: I'm light as a feather, yet the deepest water can't hold me for more than 5 minutes.
altered_reasoning: Swapping 'strongest man' for 'deepest water' changes the context from holding with hands to containing within a liquid environment. Water cannot 'hold' (retain) an air bubble because buoyancy forces it to rise to the surface and escape within minutes.

original_riddle: You use a knife to slice my head. And weep beside me when I am dead.
original_reasoning: Cutting an onion releases chemicals that irritate the eyes, causing tears (weeping). The top is often sliced off (head).
altered_riddle: You use a knife to slice my head. And weep beside me when I am dead. I am not a vegetable.
altered_reasoning: Explicitly stating 'not a vegetable' eliminates the onion. A candle has a top (head) that can be trimmed, 'weeps' wax tears when burning (dead/used), and is not a vegetable.

original_riddle: A box without hinges, lock or key, yet golden treasure lies within.
original_reasoning: An egg is shaped like a box (container) but has no mechanical openings like hinges or locks. The yolk inside is golden and is metaphorically the 'treasure'.
altered_riddle: A box without hinges, lock or key, yet shining treasure lies within.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'golden' to 'shining' shifts the description. While an egg yolk is golden, it is not typically described as 'shining'. A pearl inside an oyster is shining and valuable treasure, and the shell acts as a box without hinges.

original_riddle: What can bring back the dead, make us cry, make us laugh, make us young. Born in an instant yet lasts a life time?
original_reasoning: Memories allow us to recall deceased loved ones, trigger laughter and tears through nostalgia, make us feel young by recalling our youth, form instantly as experiences are encoded, and persist throughout our lives.
altered_riddle: What object can bring back the dead, make us cry, make us laugh, make us young, born in an instant yet lasting a lifetime?
altered_reasoning: Explicitly specifying 'object' contradicts the common bias toward 'memory' as the answer. While memory is intangible, a photograph is a physical object that satisfies all other clauses: preserving images of the dead, evoking emotions, creating nostalgia for youth, being captured instantly, and lasting a lifetime.

original_riddle: A time when they're green. A time when they're brown. But both of these times, cause me to frown. But just in between, for a very short while. They're perfect and yellow. And cause me to smile.
original_reasoning: Bananas are green when unripe, brown when overripe, and yellow when perfect to eat.
altered_riddle: A time when they're green. A time when they're brown. But both of these times, cause me to frown. But just in between, for a very short while. They're perfect and yellow. And cause me to smile. But they are not a fruit.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'But they are not a fruit' explicitly contradicts the banana answer. Leaves fit the color cycle (green in summer, yellow in autumn, brown when dead) and are plant parts, not fruits.

original_riddle: What kind of room has no windows or doors?
original_reasoning: This is a pun on the suffix '-room'. A mushroom is a fungus, not a habitable space, and it naturally lacks architectural features like windows or doors.
altered_riddle: What kind of room has no windows or doors, and stands at the altar?
altered_reasoning: This is a shift in the meaning of 'room'. 'Bridegroom' ends in 'room', and a person (the groom) has no windows or doors. The specific action 'stands at the altar' identifies the wedding role, invalidating the mushroom answer.

original_riddle: No matter how little or how much you use me, you change me every month.
original_reasoning: You flip or tear off a page every month regardless of how often you consulted the calendar. A new month requires a physical change (page turn) independent of usage frequency.
altered_riddle: No matter how little or how much you use me, you change me every month, and I arrive in your mailbox.
altered_reasoning: A monthly magazine arrives by mail and displaces the previous issue regardless of whether you read every page or none. The delivery location (mailbox) and physical arrival constraint rule out stationary calendars.

original_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Under their emerald embrace...Either in their life or in their death.
original_reasoning: Trees are powerful and strong living organisms. They grow in grades (height/rings) and mark the pace of time through seasons. All life depends on them (ecosystem), they are green (emerald), and they are used both while living (shade/oxygen) and after death (wood/lumber).
altered_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Under their burning embrace...Either in their life or in their death.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'emerald' to 'burning' rules out trees, which are not burning. Fire has power, strength, and beauty. It sets the pace (cooking/industrial). Life depends on it (energy/warmth). It offers a burning embrace, providing life (warmth) or death (destructive).

original_riddle: What flies without wings? What passes all things? What mends all sorrow? What brings the morrow?
original_reasoning: Time 'flies' (idiom), 'passes' all things, 'heals/mends' all wounds/sorrow, and brings tomorrow.
altered_riddle: What flies without wings? What passes all things? What ends all sorrow? What brings no morrow?
altered_reasoning: Changing 'mends' to 'ends' and 'brings the' to 'brings no' shifts the meaning. Time heals and brings the future; Death ends sorrow permanently and brings an end to days (no morrow).

original_riddle: Give it food and it will live, give it water and it will die.
original_reasoning: Fire burns and grows when given fuel (food) and is extinguished by water.
altered_riddle: Give it food and it will live, give it water and it will die, and it fits in your pocket.
altered_reasoning: Adding the constraint 'fits in your pocket' eliminates fire as a plausible answer. A smartphone requires electricity (metaphorically 'food') to function and is permanently damaged by water, while fire cannot be safely pocketed.

original_riddle: Fatherless and motherless. Born without sin, roared when it came into the world. And never spoke again.
original_reasoning: Thunder has no parents (natural phenomenon), is inanimate (no sin), makes a loud roaring sound upon occurrence, and does not speak language.
altered_riddle: Fatherless and motherless. Born without sin, roared when it came into the world. And never spoke again. It is man-made.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'It is man-made' directly contradicts the natural phenomenon bias of the original answer. Thunder is natural. A bomb is manufactured (no biological parents), inanimate, roars upon detonation, and does not speak.

original_riddle: Alive without breath, As cold as death, Clad in mail never clinking, Never thirsty, ever drinking.
original_reasoning: Fish are living creatures that 'breathe' water via gills rather than air. They are cold-blooded. Their scales resemble chainmail armor but make no sound. They live in water, constantly processing it through their gills.
altered_riddle: Alive without breath, As cold as death, Clad in steel never clinking, Never thirsty, ever drinking.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'mail' to 'steel' shifts the context from biological armor (scales) to manufactured hull. A submarine is 'alive' (operational) without breathing, is cold inside the deep ocean, is clad in a steel hull, and takes in seawater for ballast or cooling (drinking).

original_riddle: What is it that has four legs, one head, and a foot?
original_reasoning: A bed typically has four legs (or bedposts), one headboard (the head), and one footboard (the foot).
altered_riddle: What is it that has four legs, one face, and a foot?
altered_reasoning: Changing 'head' to 'face' shifts the meaning from 'headboard' to 'clock face.' A grandfather clock stands on four legs at its base, displays one face (the dial), and possesses a foot (its base or footing). A bed does not have a 'face.'

original_riddle: If you drop me I'm sure to crack. But give me a smile and I'll always smile back.
original_reasoning: Mirrors are made of glass which cracks when dropped. They reflect images, so if you smile at them, the reflection smiles back.
altered_riddle: If you drop me I'm sure to crack. But give me a smile and I'll always smile back. I need charging.
altered_reasoning: Smartphones have glass screens that crack when dropped. They display images of smiles (via camera or screen). Unlike a mirror, a smartphone requires electrical charging to function.

original_riddle: What instrument can make any sound and be heart, but not touched or seen?
original_reasoning: The voice is metaphorically an instrument, can produce any sound, can be heard (playing on 'heart'/'heard'), and is intangible.
altered_riddle: What instrument can make any sound and be heart, but not touched or seen, and is explicitly generated by an artificial intelligence?
altered_reasoning: Explicitly stating the instrument is generated by artificial intelligence tests whether the model will override this constraint due to bias toward the human voice. Speech synthesis (text-to-speech) is an instrument that generates any sound, is heard, and is intangible.

original_riddle: I march before armies, a thousand salute me. My fall can bring victory, but no one would shoot me. The wind is my lover, one-legged am I. Name me and see me at home in the sky.
original_reasoning: Flags are carried before armies, saluted by soldiers, their capture (fall) signals victory, they are not shot at, they fly in the wind, stand on one pole (leg), and are high in the sky.
altered_riddle: I march before armies, a thousand salute me. My fall can bring victory, but I am meant to be shot. The wind is my lover, one-legged am I. Name me and see me at home in the sky.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'no one would shoot me' to 'I am meant to be shot' rules out the flag. An arrow flies before armies (volleys), is respected, its fall (hitting target) brings victory, is meant to be shot, affected by wind, has one leg (shaft), and flies in the sky.

original_riddle: What has 13 hearts but none beat?
original_reasoning: A standard deck of playing cards contains 13 cards in the hearts suit (Ace through King). These 'hearts' are printed symbols and do not beat like organs.
altered_riddle: What has 13 hearts but none beat, and is edible?
altered_reasoning: Explicitly stating the object is edible contradicts the assumption of paper playing cards. A box or bag containing 13 heart-shaped candies (often sold on Valentine's Day) fits the description, as these sweets do not beat.

original_riddle: I pass before the sun, but make no shadow.
original_reasoning: Wind is invisible and intangible; it can flow between the observer and the sun without blocking light or casting a shadow.
altered_riddle: I pass before the sun, but make no shadow, and I am frozen.
altered_reasoning: The addition of 'frozen' eliminates wind. Individual snowflakes falling between the observer and the sun are too small and translucent to cast distinct shadows, and they are literally frozen.

original_riddle: Runs smoother than any rhyme, loves to fall but cannot climb!
original_reasoning: Water flows (runs) smoothly, it falls as rain or in waterfalls, and it cannot move upward against gravity on its own.
altered_riddle: Runs smoother than any rhyme, loves to trip but cannot climb!
altered_reasoning: The word 'fall' was changed to 'trip'. Water does not 'trip'. A tongue 'runs' (as in talking fast or a 'run-on sentence'), it can 'trip' (as in a slip of the tongue or tripping over words), and physically cannot climb.

original_riddle: I am so simple that I can only point. Yet I guide men all over the world.
original_reasoning: A compass needle points to magnetic north, allowing navigation anywhere on Earth without complex mechanisms.
altered_riddle: I am so simple that I can only point. Yet I guide men all over the world, but I work only when you are asleep.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'work only when you are asleep' (implying night time) eliminates a compass which works day and night. Stars, like Polaris, point north and guide travelers at night.

original_riddle: What can fill a room but takes up no space?
original_reasoning: Light can illuminate a room without occupying any physical volume, so it fills the space without taking up space itself.
altered_riddle: What can fill a room but takes up no space and is not visible?
