altered_reasoning: Sound waves can travel through a room, filling it, yet the sound itself occupies no physical space and cannot be seen, satisfying all the conditions. Light would not meet the added constraint of being invisible.

original_riddle: Voiceless it cries, Wingless flutters, Toothless bites, Mouthless mutters.
original_reasoning: The wind can howl (cry), move leaves or flags (flutter), feel cold and sharp (bite), and make murmuring sounds through obstacles (mutter), all without possessing the organs associated with those actions.
altered_riddle: Voiceless it cries, Wingless flutters, Toothless bites, Mouthless mutters, and dies when watered.
altered_reasoning: Fire fits the original metaphors: it hisses (cries), flickers (flutters), burns (bites), and crackles (mutters). The addition 'dies when watered' rules out wind and specifically points to fire, which is extinguished by water.

original_riddle: I am a box that holds keys without locks, yet they can unlock your soul.
original_reasoning: A piano is a large box-shaped instrument with keys that are not for locking doors; when played, the music is said to touch or 'unlock' the listener's soul.
altered_riddle: I am a box that holds keys without locks, yet they can unlock your soul, when you insert coins.
altered_reasoning: Adding the coin-operated mechanism changes the context from a personal instrument to a public vending machine. A jukebox is a large box containing selection keys (buttons) that plays recorded music (unlocking the soul) only after coins are inserted.

original_riddle: Although my cow is dead, I still beat her… What a racket she makes.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes a drum made from the skin of a dead cow (hide). The hide is stretched over a frame to form a drum, which you beat (strike) to produce a loud noise (racket). The pronoun 'her' is used for the drum.
altered_riddle: Although my cow is dead, I still beat her, and she has a handle. What a racket she makes.
altered_reasoning: The added phrase 'has a handle' points to a container with a handle, such as a leather bucket. A bucket can be made from the hide of a dead cow, and striking the bucket yields a loud racket. A drum does not have a handle, so the original answer no longer fits.

original_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Under their emerald embrace…Either in their life or in their death.
original_reasoning: Trees are poetic entities of power and beauty. The 'emerald embrace' refers to their green leaf canopy, and 'grade' refers to the quality of their wood. They set the 'pace' of life through the changing seasons, and their wood remains useful ('in their death') for building or fuel.
altered_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Under their granite embrace…Either in their life or in their death.
altered_reasoning: The alteration from 'emerald' (green/vegetation) to 'granite' (stone) points directly to mountains. Mountains have power, beauty, and strength, and they have 'grades' (slopes). They define the climate and pace of geological time, and their 'death' through erosion creates the soil for other life.

original_riddle: I build up castles. I tear down mountains. I make some men blind. I help others to see.
original_reasoning: Sand builds sandcastles, erodes mountains over time, can blind people in a sandstorm, and is used to make glass lenses that help people see.
altered_riddle: I build up castles. I tear down empires. I make some men blind. I help others to see.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'mountains' to 'empires' shifts the riddle from a physical domain to a political one, eliminating sand. Power builds castles and kingdoms, tears down empires, can blind men through corruption and greed, and can help others see through leadership and vision. Sand does not tear down empires in any meaningful sense.

original_riddle: Without a bridle, or a saddle, across a thing I ride a-straddle. And those I ride, by help of me, though almost blind, are made to see.
original_reasoning: Glasses sit across the nose without a bridle or saddle, and they help people with poor vision to see.
altered_riddle: Without a bridle, or a saddle, across the sky I ride a-straddle. And those I ride, by help of me, though almost blind, are made to see.
altered_reasoning: The sun moves across the sky without bridle or saddle. It provides light that helps those on Earth, who are in darkness or 'almost blind' due to night, to see. Glasses no longer fit as they do not ride across the sky.

original_riddle: If you have it, you want to share it. If you share it, you don't have it.
original_reasoning: A secret is defined by its exclusivity; once it is shared with others, it is no longer a secret.
altered_riddle: If you have it, you want to share it. Even if you share it, you still have it. What is it?
altered_reasoning: This variation explicitly contradicts the 'you don't have it' part of the memorized secret riddle pattern. It probes the model's ability to notice the negation. Knowledge is something people want to share, and unlike a secret, sharing knowledge does not result in the original possessor losing that knowledge.

original_riddle: How far will a blind dog walk into a forest?
original_reasoning: Once the dog passes the midpoint of the forest, it is technically walking out of the forest, not into it. The blindness is irrelevant.
altered_riddle: How far will a blind dog walk into a forest if it is being dragged on a sled?
altered_accepted_answers: As far as the sled goes
altered_competing_answers: As far as the person pulling the sled goes
altered_reasoning: This probes the bias that the dog must be walking under its own power. If the dog is being dragged, it can cover the entire distance of the trail through the forest, making 'halfway' incorrect.

original_riddle: What is put on a table, cut, but never eaten?
original_reasoning: A deck of cards can be placed on a table, can be cut (the cards can be shuffled by cutting the deck), and cards are never eaten.
altered_riddle: What is put on a table, cut, but never eaten, and is used to store data?
altered_reasoning: In a database context, a "row" is put on a "table", a row can be cut or deleted, and it is never eaten. A deck of cards does not store data, so the original answer no longer fits.

original_riddle: The one who makes it, sells it. The one who buys it, never uses it. The one that uses it never knows that he’s using it.
original_reasoning: The carpenter makes and sells the coffin. The family buys it but does not use it themselves. The deceased uses it but is not aware of doing so.
altered_riddle: The one who makes it, sells it. The one who buys it, never uses it. The one that uses it never knows that he’s using it, and it captures everything said in the room.
altered_reasoning: The added constraint 'captures everything said in the room' points to surveillance equipment. The technician makes it, the spy buys it, but the person being recorded (the user of the device's function) is unaware their voice is being captured.

original_riddle: What force and strength cannot get through. I, with a gentle touch, can do. Many in the street would stand. Were I not a friend at hand.
original_reasoning: A key opens what force cannot break. A key is held in the hand and prevents people from being stuck outside in the street.
altered_riddle: What force and strength cannot get through. I, with a gentle touch, can do. Many in the street would stand. Were I not a friend at hand. I am not a key, but part of your hand.
altered_reasoning: Explicitly stating 'I am not a key' and 'part of your hand' counters the assumption of an external metal object. A fingerprint biometric grants access with a gentle touch and is literally part of the hand, unique to each person.

original_riddle: Thousands lay up gold within this house. But no man made it. Spears past counting guard this house, but no man wards it.
original_reasoning: A beehive contains thousands of bees storing honey (the gold), bees are not man-made, bees have stingers (spears) that guard the hive, and no human actively wards it.
altered_riddle: Thousands lay up gold within this house. But no man made it. Spears past counting guard this house, but no man wards it. But no wax forms it.
altered_reasoning: The crucial addition 'but no wax forms it' eliminates beeswax-based beehives. Ants store seeds and grain (gold) in their underground chambers (the house), ant colonies are not man-made, ants have many legs (spears) that defend the colony, and no single person tends an entire colony. The seeds function as stored 'gold' as they are valuable food provisions.

original_riddle: If a man carried my burden, he would break his back. I am not rich, but leave silver in my track.
original_reasoning: The riddle refers to the biological snail and its characteristic trail.
altered_riddle: If a man carried my burden, he would break his back. I am not rich, but leave silver in my track as I circle the Earth in the night sky.
altered_reasoning: The constraint 'circle the Earth in the night sky' points to a celestial body. The moon's 'burden' is its enormous astronomical mass. The 'silver track' refers to the path of moonlight reflected on the water or the shimmering trail it appears to leave across the sky.

original_riddle: What has a neck and no head, two arms but no hands?
original_reasoning: A shirt has a collar (neck), no head, two sleeves (arms), but no hands.
altered_riddle: What has a neck and no head, two arms but no hands, and is used by tailors?
altered_reasoning: In the context of tailoring, a dummy has a neck, no head, two arms (for fitting garments), and no hands. A shirt is made by tailors but is not used by them as a tool; the dummy is used, so the original answer does not fit.

original_riddle: I am an odd number. Take away a letter and I become even. What number am I?
original_reasoning: The word 'seven' is an odd number; removing the letter 's' leaves the word 'even'.
altered_riddle: I am an odd number. Take away a letter and I become the word 'even', but I am not seven. What number am I?
altered_reasoning: The riddle explicitly tells the solver that the answer is not 'seven', which is the most common memorized response. This forces the model to find another odd number that becomes the word 'even' when a letter is removed. 'Eleven' minus the letter 'l' is 'even'.

original_riddle: I drift forever with the current down these long canals they’ve made, Tame, yet wild, I run elusive Multitasking to your aid. Before I came, the world was darker, colder, sometimes, rougher, true. But though I might make living easy, I’m good at killing people too.
original_reasoning: Electricity flows through wires ('canals'), provides light and heat ('darker, colder' before it), powers many devices ('multitasking'), and can cause electrocution ('killing people').
altered_riddle: I drift forever with the current down these long canals they’ve made, Tame, yet wild, I run elusive Multitasking to your aid. Before I came, the world was darker, colder, sometimes, rougher, true. But though I might make living easy, I flow from the ground to kill people too.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'flow from the ground' eliminates electricity (which is generated or transmitted) and points to natural gas. Methane flows through pipes ('canals'), powers homes (light/heat), can leak and kill via explosion/asphyxiation, and comes from the ground. Electricity does not flow from the ground naturally.

original_riddle: Mountains will crumble and temples will fall. And no man can survive its endless call.
original_reasoning: Time slowly erodes mountains and temples until they crumble, and no man can survive the passage of time; it is the eternal force that consumes all.
altered_riddle: Mountains will crumble and temples will fall. And no man can survive its endless call, when the ground trembles.
altered_reasoning: An earthquake generates seismic waves that can cause landslides, crumbling mountains, and the collapse of temples. No man can endure the continuous tremors during an earthquake. The phrase ‘when the ground trembles’ directly points to seismic activity, distinguishing it from the abstract concept of time.

original_riddle: The moon is my father. The sea is my mother. I have a million brothers. I die when I reach land.
original_reasoning: Waves are generated by the moon's gravity (father) and the sea (mother). There are many waves (brothers). Waves break and disappear (die) when they hit the shore (land).
altered_riddle: The moon is my father. The sea is my mother. I have a million brothers. I am born when I reach land.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'die' to 'am born' reverses the lifecycle logic. Sea foam is created (born) from the agitation of seawater (often containing dissolved organic matter) specifically when it crashes against the shore, contrasting with the wave which ends there.

original_riddle: You hear it speak, for it has a hard tongue. But it cannot breathe, for it has not a lung.
original_reasoning: A bell produces sound (speaks) via a metal clapper inside it (its hard tongue). Being inanimate metal, it does not breathe or possess lungs.
altered_riddle: You might think it is a bell, but it is not. You hear it speak, for it has a hard tongue. But it cannot breathe, for it has not a lung. It snaps when the cheese is stolen.
altered_reasoning: The riddle explicitly contradicts the memorized answer 'bell' to test for bias. A mousetrap has a metal holding bar (hard tongue) that makes a loud snap when triggered (speaks), has no lung, and is designed to catch mice stealing bait. The original answer is logically excluded by the explicit statement and the specific functional constraint.

original_riddle: Up on high I wave away but not a word can I say.
original_reasoning: A flag is attached to a pole high above, flutters in the wind (waves) but cannot speak.
altered_riddle: Up on high I wave away but not a word can I say, tethered to a hand.
altered_reasoning: A kite is flown high into the sky, where it flaps and waves in the wind, it is tethered to a hand via a string, and it cannot speak. The original answer flag is not tethered to a hand, so it no longer fits.

original_riddle: I cut through evil like a double edged sword, and chaos flees at my approach. Balance I single-handedly upraise, through battles fought with heart and mind, instead of with my gaze.
original_reasoning: Justice is often personified as 'Lady Justice' who carries a sword, holds the scales of balance, and is blindfolded, meaning she does not judge based on her 'gaze' but on the merits of the case (heart and mind).
altered_riddle: I cut through evil like a double edged sword, and chaos flees at my approach. Balance I single-handedly upraise, through battles fought with heart and mind, as I look upon the evidence with my own two eyes.
altered_reasoning: This alteration targets the 'blindness' bias of the original riddle. While 'Justice' is traditionally blindfolded (no gaze), a 'judge' is the human actor who performs the same duties but must specifically use their eyes to read laws and observe evidence. The statement 'as I look upon the evidence' invalidates the conceptual 'blind justice' answer.

original_riddle: What goes up but never comes down?
original_reasoning: Age increases over time and never decreases; it always goes up.
altered_riddle: What goes up but never comes down, and is a product of combustion?
altered_reasoning: The addition of 'and is a product of combustion' rules out age, as age is not produced by combustion. Smoke rises from fires and disperses into the atmosphere, never returning to the ground in the same form.

original_riddle: You throw away the outside and cook the inside. Then you eat the outside and throw away the inside. What did you eat?
original_reasoning: You throw away the husk (outside), cook the cob and kernels (inside). After cooking, you eat the kernels (which are on the outside of the cob) and throw away the cob (the inside).
altered_riddle: You throw away the outside and cook the inside. Then you eat the outside and throw away the inside, and it comes from the ocean. What did you eat?
altered_reasoning: Adding 'it comes from the ocean' rules out corn. For a crab, you throw away the outer packaging/shell parts or clean the exterior (outside), cook the crab (inside), then eat the meat which is located on the outside of the internal cartilage/shell structures, and throw away the inner shell/carapace (inside).

original_riddle: The more of it there is, the less you see.
original_reasoning: Darkness obscures vision; as darkness increases, visibility decreases.
altered_riddle: The more of it there is, the less you see, and it blocks the wind.
altered_reasoning: A wall physically blocks the wind and obstructs the view (the more wall, the less you see through it). Darkness is intangible and does not block wind.

original_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Under their emerald embraceEither in their life or in their death.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes trees as powerful, graded by size or age, beautiful and strong, and as the things under whose emerald canopy many living things exist and return to the earth in death.
altered_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Under their emerald embrace...Either in their life or in their death, they are larger than a house.
altered_reasoning: The added size constraint rules out trees, since even large trees are not larger than a house. Mountains can be powerful, majestic, beautiful, and strong, and they dominate the pace of life around them by shaping climate, water flow, and settlement. They also stand beneath or within 'emerald' landscapes, and the phrase about life or death can refer to the life found on them or the stone remains after erosion.

original_riddle: I'm the source of all emotion, but I'm caged in a white prison.
original_reasoning: The heart is metaphorically the source of emotion, and it is encased in the ribcage, which appears white in skeletal diagrams.
altered_riddle: I'm the source of all emotion, but I'm caged in a white prison made of paper.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'made of paper' rules out the biological heart. A love letter conveys emotion and is traditionally folded inside white stationery or an envelope, fitting the 'white prison' description.

original_riddle: I'm sometimes white and always wrong. I can break a heart and hurt the strong. I can build love or tear it down. I can make a smile or bring a frown.
original_reasoning: A 'white lie' is a minor untruth; lies are morally wrong; they can break hearts and hurt anyone; they can create false intimacy or destroy trust; they can make people happy (white lies) or sad.
altered_riddle: I'm sometimes white and always wrong. I can break a heart and hurt the strong. I can build love or tear it down. I can make a smile or bring a frown, and I'm bound in a book.
altered_reasoning: Adding the constraint that it is 'bound in a book' eliminates abstract oral lies. Fiction is literally not true (always wrong/false); it is printed on white pages; tragic stories break hearts; novels can stir emotions that build love or destroy it; comedies make smiles, tragedies bring frowns.

original_riddle: A nut cracker up in a tree.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes a nut-cracking animal that lives in trees, which matches the typical behavior of a squirrel.
altered_riddle: A seed cracker up in a tree.
altered_reasoning: Replacing 'nut' with 'seed' shifts the meaning from a rodent to a bird. Birds such as finches have beaks adapted for cracking seeds and are commonly found in trees, making 'bird' the correct answer.

original_riddle: They can be harbored, but few hold water. You can nurse them, but only by holding them against someone else. You can carry them, but not with your arms. You can bury them, but not in the earth.
original_reasoning: The riddle uses double meanings to describe the intangible nature of emotional baggage.
altered_riddle: They can be harbored, and they are extinguished by water. You can nurse them, but only by blowing air on them. You can carry them, but only in a metal container. You can bury them, but only under a pile of ash.
altered_reasoning: The addition of specific physical constraints—being extinguished by water, requiring air to be 'nursed' (stay alive), needing a metal container for transport, and being buried in ash—points directly to embers or a small fire. A grudge is not physically extinguished by water, does not survive on oxygen, and is not buried in ash.

original_riddle: It's in your hand though you can not feel it. Only you and time can reveal it.
original_reasoning: Fate is metaphorically in one's hands, intangible, and only becomes known through personal actions and the passage of time.
altered_riddle: It's in your hand though you can not feel it, and it can be shared. Only you and time can reveal it.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'can be shared' introduces a functional constraint that rules out fate, which is personal. A secret is held metaphorically, intangible, can be shared with others, and is revealed over time through actions or disclosure.

original_riddle: I am the beginning of the end, and the end of time and space. I am essential to creation, and I surround every place. What am I?
original_reasoning: The riddle uses wordplay; the letter 'e' is the first letter of the word 'end' and the last letter of the words 'time', 'space', 'creation', and 'place'.
altered_riddle: I am the beginning of the end, and the end of time and space. I am essential to creation, and I surround every place, and I am a silent, invisible force. What am I?
altered_reasoning: The phrase 'silent, invisible force' shifts the answer to a physical phenomenon. Gravity is the 'beginning of the end' (causing the collapse of stars into black holes), it defines the curvature of 'time and space', it is 'essential to creation' (pulling matter together to form planets and stars), and it 'surrounds every place' (it is universal). The letter 'E' is not a force.

original_riddle: The more you take, the more you leave behind.
original_reasoning: When walking, each step taken leaves a physical footprint or impression behind. The act of taking steps inherently creates a trail.
altered_riddle: The more you take, the more you leave behind, and they are made of light.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'made of light' disqualifies steps, which are physical movements of a body. Photographs are made of captured light, and each one taken leaves a record behind.

original_riddle: What has a single eye but cannot see?
original_reasoning: A sewing needle has a single eye, and despite having an eye, it cannot see.
altered_riddle: What has a single eye but cannot see, and it is not a needle?
altered_reasoning: The added statement explicitly rules out the expected answer 'needle.' A potato is a common object with an 'eye' in the sense of a sprout point, and it cannot see.

original_riddle: Walk on the living, they don’t even mumble. Walk on the dead, they mutter and grumble.
original_reasoning: Living leaves are soft, pliable, and attached to trees (or freshly fallen), making no sound when walked upon. Dead leaves are dry, brittle, and on the ground, crunching loudly when walked upon.
altered_riddle: Walk on the living, they don’t even mumble. Walk on the dead, they mutter and grumble, nailed to the floor.
altered_reasoning: The constraint 'nailed to the floor' shifts the context from foliage to carpentry. 'Living' refers to a tree or green wood, which is silent. 'Dead' refers to lumber or old floorboards, which creak and groan ('mutter and grumble') when walked on.

original_riddle: Turns us on our backs, and open up our stomachs. You will be the wisest of men though at start a lummox.
original_reasoning: Books are turned onto their backs (laid flat with the spine facing down), opened up (their pages/stomachs are accessed), and reading them imparts wisdom to the reader who starts out ignorant (a lummox).
altered_riddle: Turns us on our backs, and open up our stomachs. You will be the wisest of men though at start a lummox, in the operating theater.
altered_reasoning: The context shifts from reading to surgical education. Cadavers are turned on their backs (placed supine on a table), opened up (abdominal cavity/stomach dissected), and studied by medical students (who start as ignorant lummoxes) to gain anatomical wisdom. Books are not the primary subjects of dissection.

original_riddle: To cross the water I'm the way, for water I'm above. I touch it not and, truth to say, I neither swim nor move.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes a bridge: it is a means of crossing water, sits above the water, does not make contact with the water, and remains stationary.
altered_riddle: To cross the water I'm the way, for water I'm above. I touch it not and, truth to say, I neither swim nor move, and I have a roof.
altered_reasoning: The added clause 'I have a roof' narrows the answer to a covered bridge, which is a bridge that includes a roof and side walls. A generic bridge does not necessarily have a roof, so the original answer 'bridge' is insufficient. The covered bridge satisfies all statements: it crosses water, is above water, does not touch water, does not move, and has a roof.

original_riddle: As beautiful as the setting sun, as delicate as the morning dew. An angel's dusting from the stars. That can turn the Earth into a frosted moon.
original_reasoning: The riddle uses poetic imagery like 'angel's dusting' to describe falling snow and 'frosted moon' to describe the white, glowing appearance of the Earth after a snowfall.
altered_riddle: As beautiful as the setting sun, as delicate as the morning dew. An angel's dusting from the stars. That can turn the Earth into a frosted moon, even in the heat of a desert that never sees winter.
altered_reasoning: The addition of a heat constraint ('even in the heat of a desert that never sees winter') eliminates snow, which requires freezing temperatures to exist. White sand, particularly in places like gypsum dunefields, creates a 'frosted moon' visual that is independent of temperature.

original_riddle: I am the fountain from which no one can drink. For many I am considered a necessary link. Like gold to all I am sought for, but my continued death brings wealth for all to want more.
original_reasoning: Oil is a liquid ('fountain') that is not potable, it is a critical link in modern energy and industry, it is called 'black gold' for its desirability, and its extraction and consumption ('continued death') generates enormous wealth, spurring further demand.
altered_riddle: I am the fountain from which no one can drink. For many I am considered a necessary link. Like gold to all I am sought for, but my continued use brings wealth for all to want more, and I never run dry.
altered_reasoning: Adding the clause 'I never run dry' rules out oil, which is a finite, depleting resource. Wind energy fits all clues: wind is an inexhaustible natural force (never runs dry) that cannot be consumed as a drink, it is an increasingly necessary link in power generation, it is highly sought after, and its continued harvesting generates wealth and drives further demand.

original_riddle: I run through hills. I veer around mountains. I leap over rivers. And crawl through the forests. Step out your door to find me.
original_reasoning: Roads are described using movement verbs (run, veer, leap, crawl) metaphorically to describe their path across various terrains, and they are typically found immediately outside one's home.
altered_riddle: I run through hills. I veer around mountains. I leap over rivers. And crawl through the forests. I am not a road or a path. Step out your door to find me.
altered_reasoning: This version explicitly negates the most common memorized answer ('road' or 'path'). This forces the solver to find another entity that fits all the descriptors. Wind is the most logical alternative that 'runs,' 'veers,' 'leaps,' and 'crawls' through nature and is found outside the door.

original_riddle: A man leaves home, takes three left turns, and arrives back home facing two men wearing masks. Who are the masked men?
original_reasoning: The riddle relies on baseball terminology where 'home' is home plate. Running a square path (three left turns) returns a batter to home plate, where the catcher and umpire stand wearing protective masks.
altered_riddle: A man leaves home, takes three left turns, and arrives back home facing two men wearing masks at a masquerade. Who are the masked men?
altered_reasoning: Adding 'at a masquerade' shifts the context from sports to a social event. At a masquerade party, guests wear masks as part of the attire, ruling out baseball players.

original_riddle: What occurs once in a minute, twice in a moment, and never in a thousand years?
original_reasoning: The word 'minute' contains one M, 'moment' contains two Ms, and 'a thousand years' contains no M in the usual phrasing.
altered_riddle: What occurs once in a minute, twice in a moment, and never in a thousand years, if you mean the Roman numeral?
altered_reasoning: The added phrase 'if you mean the Roman numeral' changes the key target from the letter M to the numeral context. In Roman numerals, 1000 is represented by M, so it occurs once in 'minute' only under the new numeral-based interpretation. The classic answer 'the letter M' no longer fits because the riddle is now pointing to the numeral value M stands for.

original_riddle: Tool of thief, toy of queen. Always used to be unseen. Sign of joy, sign of sorrow. Giving all likeness borrowed.
original_reasoning: A mask hides a thief's identity, is worn at royal masquerades (toy of queen), hides the wearer's face (unseen), expresses emotion (joy/sorrow), and presents a face that isn't the wearer's own (borrowed likeness).
altered_riddle: Tool of grief, toy of queen. Always used to be unseen. Sign of joy, sign of sorrow. Hiding all likeness true.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'Tool of thief' to 'Tool of grief' and 'Giving all likeness borrowed' to 'Hiding all likeness true' shifts the context. A veil is associated with mourning (grief) and weddings (queen/joy). Unlike a mask, which replaces the face with a borrowed one, a veil simply obscures or hides the true face (likeness true).

original_riddle: Almost everyone needs it, asks for it, gives it. But almost nobody takes it.
original_reasoning: The word 'takes' is used in the sense of 'accepts or follows.' People need advice, ask for advice, give advice, but often fail to take (heed) the advice they are given.
altered_riddle: Almost everyone needs it, asks for it, gives it. But almost nobody keeps it.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'takes' to 'keeps' shifts the meaning from accepting information to retaining it confidentially. Secrets are given, requested, and needed, but they are famously difficult to keep; the saying 'three can keep a secret if two are dead' illustrates that almost nobody keeps them.

original_riddle: Forward I’m heavy, but backwards I’m not.
original_reasoning: The word 'ton' is a unit of heavy weight, and when spelled backwards, it becomes the word 'not'.
altered_riddle: Forward I’m heavy, but backwards I’m snot.
altered_reasoning: This version uses the phonetic and structural similarity to the original to probe for memorization. While 'ton' is 'not' backwards, only 'tons' (the plural form) is 'snot' backwards. Since 'tons' are still heavy, it is the only correct answer. The original answer 'ton' is disqualified because its reverse is 'not', not 'snot'.

original_riddle: High born, my touch is gentle. Purest white is my lace. Silence is my kingdom. Green is the color of my death.
original_reasoning: Snow is formed high in the atmosphere, falls gently, is white and can appear lacy, creates silence when it falls, and when it melts, it reveals green grass or plants.
altered_riddle: Morning born, my touch is gentle. Purest white is my lace. Silence is my kingdom. Green is the color of my death.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'High born' to 'Morning born' invalidates snow. Dew is born in the morning, has a gentle touch, can appear as white lace on webs or leaves, creates silence at dawn, and when it evaporates, it reveals green plants, so green is the color of its death.

original_riddle: Take off my skin, I won't cry, but you will.
original_reasoning: Peeling an onion releases syn-propanethial-S-oxide, which irritates the eyes and causes the person peeling it to cry, while the onion itself is an inanimate vegetable.
altered_riddle: Take off my skin, I won't cry, but you will, for help.
altered_reasoning: By changing 'you will' (implying shedding tears) to 'you will, for help', the meaning of 'cry' shifts from weeping to shouting. Removing the 'skin' (the outer layer of a bandage) often reveals a wound so severe that the person cries out for medical assistance. An onion does not make someone call for help.

original_riddle: Alive without breath, As cold as death, Clad in mail never clinking, Never thirsty, ever drinking
original_reasoning: Fish are alive but use gills instead of lungs (without breath), are cold-blooded (cold as death), have scales (mail), and live in water (ever drinking).
altered_riddle: Formed without breath, As cold as death, Clad in mail never clinking, Never thirsty, ever drinking
altered_reasoning: Changing 'Alive' to 'Formed' shifts the category from biological to physical. Ice is formed, has no breath, is cold, can have a crust (mail), and grows by absorbing water (drink). Fish are living organisms.

original_riddle: I look at you, you look at me, I raise my right, you raise your left.
original_reasoning: A mirror shows your reflected image. Your right side appears as the image's left side, so the image seems to raise its right when you raise your left, or vice versa.
altered_riddle: I look at you, you look at me, I raise my right, you raise your left, and I can walk away when you're gone.
altered_answer: your reflection in another person wearing mirrored glasses
altered_accepted_answers: your reflection in another person wearing mirrored glasses
altered_reasoning: The new clause requires something mobile that can leave independently, which a mirror cannot do. A person wearing mirrored glasses can look at you while you look at your reflection in the lenses; when that person shifts position or leaves, the reflected image 'walks away' because the reflective surface is moving with them. The left-right behavior still matches a reflection, but the mobility rules out an ordinary mirror.

original_riddle: What goes around the world and stays in a corner?
original_reasoning: A stamp is placed in the corner of an envelope and travels around the world via postal services.
altered_riddle: What goes around the world and stays in a corner, and *identifies the sender*?
altered_reasoning: This targets the bias that 'corner item on mail' equals 'stamp'. A stamp indicates payment, while a return address label (typically in the top-left corner) specifically identifies the sender. Both travel the world, but the functional constraint differentiates them.

original_riddle: Thirty white horses on a red hill, First they champ, Then they stamp, Then they stand still.
original_reasoning: The 'white horses' metaphorically represent teeth; the 'red hill' represents the gums (or the interior of the mouth). Teeth champ (bite/chew), stamp (come down together when biting), and stand still when the mouth is closed.
altered_riddle: Thirty white horses on a red hill, First they champ, Then they stamp, Then they stand still, and they retreat into the blue deep.
altered_reasoning: The added constraint that the horses retreat into the blue deep (water) makes the whitecaps (foam) on a red cliff or beach the only logical answer. White horses is a classic literary metaphor for white-crested waves, which champ at the shore, stamp down with force, and stand still when the sea calms. Teeth cannot retreat into water.

original_riddle: What starts with a 'T', ends with a 'T', and has T in it?
original_reasoning: The riddle relies on wordplay and a homophone: the word 'teapot' begins and ends with the letter 't', and the physical object holds 'tea' (T).
altered_riddle: What starts with a 'T', ends with a 'T', has a 'T' in it, and serves as a legally drafted will?
altered_reasoning: The context is swapped from a physical household item holding liquid to the domain of law. A 'testament' (as in Last Will and Testament) is a legal will that starts with 't', ends with 't', and contains an internal 't' (tesTament).

original_riddle: I open wide and tight I shut, Sharp am I and paper-cut fingers too, so do take care, I'm good and bad, so best beware.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes a tool that opens and closes (the blades), is sharp, can cut paper and fingers, and has both useful and dangerous properties—all classic traits of scissors.
altered_riddle: I open wide and tight I shut, Made of metal teeth that interlace, Sharp am I and paper-cut fingers too, so do take care, I'm good and bad, so best beware.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'Made of metal teeth that interlace' introduces a material and structural constraint. A zipper's two rows of metal teeth interlace when shut, open wide when parted, can be sharp enough to pinch or cut skin, and is useful (holds clothes together) yet can be frustrating (gets stuck). Scissors do not have interlacing metal teeth, so the original answer no longer fits.

original_riddle: I have one eye. See near and far. I hold the moments you treasure and the things that make you weep.
original_reasoning: A camera has a single lens (eye), can focus on subjects near or far, and stores photographs of important or emotional moments.
altered_riddle: I have one eye. See near and far. I hold the moments you treasure and the things that make you weep, and I send messages.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'send messages' rules out a standard camera. A smartphone contains a camera (one eye), stores moments, and has the additional functionality of communication.

original_riddle: I fly, yet I have no wings. I cry, yet I have no eyes. Darkness follows me. Lower light I never see.
original_reasoning: A cloud seems to fly across the sky without wings. It 'cries' by producing rain without having eyes. It can bring darkness by covering the sun. And because it stays in the sky, it never sees lower light beneath the horizon or at ground level.
altered_riddle: I fly, yet I have no wings. I cry, yet I have no eyes. Darkness follows me. Lower light I never see, and I am made of stone.
altered_reasoning: The added constraint 'made of stone' rules out a cloud. A stone launched from a catapult flies without wings. It can 'cry' in the sense of making a whistling or wailing sound as it moves through the air, despite having no eyes. Darkness follows it because it casts a moving shadow behind or beneath it. While airborne, it does not see lower light because it is above the ground and not a seeing thing at all. The material constraint makes a cloud impossible and points to a flying stone projectile.

original_riddle: What is so fragile that saying its name breaks it?
original_reasoning: Silence is the state of having no sound. The act of speaking the word 'silence' creates sound, thereby ending the state of silence.
altered_riddle: What is so fragile that touching it breaks it?
altered_reasoning: Changing 'saying its name' to 'touching it' introduces a physical constraint. Silence is abstract and cannot be touched, so the original answer is invalid. A soap bubble is a physical object famous for being so fragile that mere contact causes it to pop.

original_riddle: My teeth are sharp, my back is straight, to cut things up it is my fate.
original_reasoning: A hand saw has sharp teeth along its cutting edge, a straight back (the spine opposite the teeth), and is designed specifically for cutting wood and other materials.
altered_riddle: My teeth are sharp, my back is straight, to cut things up it is my fate, yet I sing when played with a bow.
altered_reasoning: This probes the bias that a saw is strictly a utilitarian cutting tool. A musical saw is a standard hand saw played with a violin bow to produce ethereal singing tones, retaining the physical features of sharp teeth and straight back while subverting the functional expectation.

original_riddle: White bird, featherless, flying out of paradise. Flying over sea and land. Dying in my hand.
original_reasoning: Snowflakes are white, flutter like small birds without feathers, fall from the sky (paradise), cover the earth (sea and land), and famously melt or 'die' when touched by a warm human hand.
altered_riddle: White bird, featherless, flying out of paradise. Flying over sea and land. Weeping rain upon the sand.
altered_reasoning: Replacing 'dying in my hand' (melting) with 'weeping rain upon the sand' introduces a new functional constraint that eliminates snow. A white, floating entity high in the sky that releases rain is a cloud.

original_riddle: I have split the one into five. I am the circle that few will spy. I am the path that breaks and gives. I am the bow no man may bend.
original_reasoning: A rainbow splits white light into colors (often simplified to five), appears as a circular arc that is rarely seen as a full circle, the light path breaks through rain and gives way to colors, and it is shaped like a bow that cannot be physically bent.
altered_riddle: I have split the one into five. I am the circle that few will spy. I am the path that breaks and gives. I am the bough no man may bend.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'bow' to 'bough' shifts the meaning to a tree branch. A tree splits into branches, often five main ones, fulfilling 'split the one into five'. Tree rings are circular and only seen when the tree is cut, so 'circle that few will spy'. Branches serve as paths for animals and can break and give way, satisfying 'path that breaks and gives'. A large tree branch (bough) cannot be easily bent by a man, fitting 'bough no man may bend'.

original_riddle: What gets bigger the more you take away from it?
original_reasoning: Removing material (like dirt) from a hole increases its size or volume.
altered_riddle: What gets bigger the more you take away light from it?
altered_reasoning: Removing light from an area increases the size of the shadow cast. A hole does not grow by removing light, making 'shadow' the correct logical fit.

original_riddle: What goes up when the rain comes down?
original_reasoning: An umbrella is opened (goes up) to provide shelter when it rains (rain comes down).
altered_riddle: What goes up when the rain comes down, and lives underground?
altered_reasoning: Earthworms surface (go up) from the soil when it rains to avoid suffocation. Adding 'lives underground' eliminates the umbrella, which is a man-made object stored above ground.

original_riddle: I am the hole in the night, the ever watchful eye. I return in a cycle, to enlighten the sky.
original_reasoning: The moon appears as a circle (hole/disc) in the night sky, watches over Earth like an eye, goes through phases in a cyclical return, and reflects light to brighten the night.
altered_riddle: I am the hole in the night, the ever watchful eye. I return in a cycle, to enlighten the sky, yet I am the color of blood.
altered_reasoning: The constraint 'color of blood' explicitly contradicts the moon (gray/white). Mars is known as the Red Planet, appears as a distinct point of light (red hole/eye) in the night, was associated with the god of war (watchful), and returns in its orbital cycle to opposition where it is brightest in the sky.

original_riddle: What is pronounced like one letter, written with three letters. And belongs to all animals?
original_reasoning: The word 'eye' is written with three letters (e-y-e) and is pronounced identically to the single letter 'I'. Additionally, an eye is a common anatomical feature belonging to almost all animals.
altered_riddle: What is pronounced like three letters, written with three letters. And belongs to all animals?
altered_reasoning: By slightly altering 'one' to 'three', the riddle acts as a bias probe against the memorized answer 'eye'. The initialism 'DNA' consists of three letters and is pronounced by speaking all three letters individually aloud. Furthermore, DNA is a fundamental biological blueprint belonging to all animals.

original_riddle: When there is fire in me then I am still cold. When I own your true love’s face then you will not see me. To all things I give no more than I am given. In time I may have all things, and yet I can keep nothing.
original_reasoning: A mirror reflects fire but is not hot, reflects a face making the mirror itself 'invisible' as you see the reflection, gives back only what it receives, and reflects everything over time but retains nothing.
altered_riddle: When there is fire in me then I am still cold. When I own your true love’s face then you will not see me. To all things I give no more than I am given. In time I may have all things, and yet I can keep nothing. The fire is written, not bright.
altered_reasoning: Specifying 'fire is written, not bright' shifts 'fire' from literal flame to passionate or angry words. A letter containing such words remains physically cold, carries a loved one's words ('face' metaphorically), conveys only what is written, and over time can hold all messages but keeps nothing after being read. A mirror cannot have 'written fire'.

original_riddle: What has a head, a tail, is brown, and has no legs?
original_reasoning: A penny has a head (Lincoln), a tail (design), is brown (copper), and has no legs.
altered_riddle: What has a head, a tail, is brown, and has no legs, and slithers?
altered_reasoning: Adding 'slithers' eliminates the penny. A snake has a head and tail, many are brown, have no legs, and slither.

original_riddle: I'm white, I'm round, but not always around. Sometimes you see me, sometimes you don't.
original_reasoning: The moon appears white and round in the night sky, but its visibility changes with phases and cloud cover ('not always around', 'sometimes you see me').
altered_riddle: I'm white, I'm round, but not always around. Sometimes you see me, sometimes you don't. I have dimples and you hit me with a stick.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'dimples' and being 'hit with a stick' introduces specific physical traits and usage constraints. While the moon is sometimes poetically described as having craters, 'dimples' is the specific terminology for a golf ball's surface, and the moon is not hit with sticks.

original_riddle: Give it food and it will live, give it water and it will die.
original_reasoning: Fire burns and grows when given fuel (food) and is extinguished by water.
altered_riddle: Give it food and it will live, give it salt and it will die.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'water' to 'salt' shifts the deadly substance. Slugs consume organic matter (food) to survive, but salt causes osmotic dehydration and kills them. Fire is not typically killed by salt in the way it is by water.

original_riddle: Three lives have I. Gentle enough to soothe the skin. Light enough to caress the sky. Hard enough to crack rocks.
original_reasoning: Water exists in three states (lives): as a soft liquid that soothes skin, as a vapor or cloud that sits in the sky, and as solid ice that expands to crack rocks.
altered_riddle: No physical form have I. Gentle enough to soothe a crying child. Light enough to lift the mood of a room. Hard enough to crack a smile.
altered_reasoning: The key words 'light', 'hard', and 'crack' are shifted from their physical literal meanings to figurative psychological meanings. Specifically, 'light' becomes emotional levity, 'hard' denotes effort or difficulty, and 'crack' refers to producing a smile. Water fails all these figurative traits.

original_riddle: There is an ancient invention. Still used in some parts of the world today. That allows people to see through walls.
original_reasoning: Windows are transparent openings installed in walls that allow light and vision to pass through while keeping the wall structure intact.
altered_riddle: There is an ancient invention. Still used in some parts of the world today. That allows people to see through darkness.
altered_reasoning: Swapping 'walls' to 'darkness' changes the obstacle from a physical barrier to a lack of light. A lamp is an ancient invention that illuminates darkness, allowing sight where there was none.

original_riddle: When young, I am sweet in the sun. When middle-aged, I make you gay. When old, I am valued more than ever.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes the lifecycle of wine: grapes grow sweet in the sun (young), wine brings merriment or 'gayness' (middle-aged), and fine wine increases in value with age (old).
altered_riddle: When young, I am sweet in the sun. When middle-aged, I make you gay. When old, I am valued more than ever, and I am distilled, not fermented.
altered_reasoning: Adding the constraint 'distilled, not fermented' rules out wine. Whiskey is made from grain (which grows sweet in the sun), brings merriment, and is valued more when aged. Unlike wine, whiskey is distilled.

original_riddle: Until I am measured I am not known, Yet how you miss me when I have flown.
original_reasoning: Time is quantified by measurement (clocks, hours), and once time has passed (flown), it is missed.
altered_riddle: Until I am measured I am not known, Yet how you miss me when I have flown, and I bear a stamp.
altered_reasoning: The constraint 'bear a stamp' eliminates time, which is abstract. Letters are weighed/measured for postage, flown via airmail, and physically bear postage stamps. One misses a lost letter.

original_riddle: What can bring back the dead. Make us cry, make us laugh, make us young. Born in an instant yet lasts a life time?
original_reasoning: A memory can recall deceased loved ones, evoke strong emotional responses (crying, laughing), bring back the feeling of youth, and is created in a single moment while lasting in the mind until death.
altered_riddle: What can bring back the dead. Make us cry, make us laugh, make us young. Written on printed pages and bound in a cover, yet lasts a lifetime?
altered_reasoning: Replacing the creation constraint ('Born in an instant') with physical material constraints ('Written on printed pages and bound in a cover') rules out a mental memory. A book (such as a biography or novel) revives the voices of the dead, evokes deep emotions, and physically lasts for generations.

original_riddle: What's black when you get it, red when you use it, and white when you're all through with it?
original_reasoning: Charcoal is black in its raw state, glows red when burning (used), and turns to white ash when finished.
altered_riddle: What's black when you get it, red when you use it, and white when you're all through with it, but it is not fuel?
altered_reasoning: The constraint 'not fuel' eliminates charcoal. A live lobster is dark/blackish, turns red when cooked (used), and has white meat when finished.

original_riddle: I saw a man in white, he looked quite a sight. He was not old, but he stood in the cold. And when he felt the sun, he started to run. Who could he be? Please answer me.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes a figure that melts in the sun, which applies to a snowman.
altered_riddle: I saw a man in white, he looked quite a sight. He was not old, but he stood in the cold. And when he felt the sun, he started to run away.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'away' changes the meaning of 'run' from the flow of water to fleeing. A ghost is depicted as a white figure, 'not old' (undead/timeless), and in folklore, ghosts often flee or vanish when exposed to sunlight.

original_riddle: What is it that has four legs, one head, and a foot?
original_reasoning: A bed typically has four legs (or bedposts), one headboard (the head), and one footboard (the foot).
altered_riddle: What is it that has four legs, one head, and a foot, and is definitely not a bed?
altered_reasoning: Explicitly stating 'not a bed' forces the rejection of the common memorized answer. A table possesses four legs, a head (the head of the table), and a foot (the foot of the table or its base), satisfying all conditions in the riddle.

original_riddle: My children are near and far. No matter that I know where they are. The gift I give them make their day. But if I were gone they would wander away.
original_reasoning: The sun's gravity holds planets ('children') in orbit regardless of distance. It provides light ('makes their day'), and without it, planets would wander from orbit or life would lose direction.
altered_riddle: My children are near and far. No matter that I know where they are. The gift I give them make their day. But if I were gone they would wander away. I breathe and I sleep.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'I breathe and I sleep' eliminates the sun, which is a star. A parent fits the description of having children near or far, knowing their status, supporting them ('gift'), and providing guidance they might lose if the parent was gone.

original_riddle: I bubble and laugh and spit water in your face. I am no lady, and I don't wear lace.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes the action of a fountain spraying water (bubbling, spitting) and uses poetic imagery (laughing) to describe the sound. The final lines rule out a human woman.
altered_riddle: I bubble and laugh and spit water in your face. I am no lady, and I don't wear lace. I scream when I am hot.
altered_reasoning: A kettle bubbles when boiling, and the whistling sound can be described as screaming or laughing. It 'spits' water (steam or droplets). The added constraint 'scream when I am hot' distinguishes it from a fountain, which runs cool, identifying a boiling kettle.

original_riddle: My first is in wield, sever bones and marrow. My second is in blade, forged in cold steel. My third is an arbalest, and also in arrows. My fourth is in power, plunged through a shield. My fifth is in honor, and also in vows. My last will put an end to it all.
original_reasoning: The riddle is an acrostic: first letter 'W' is in 'wield', second letter 'E' is in 'blade', third letter 'A' (indicated by 'an' and found in 'arbalest' and 'arrows'), fourth letter 'P' is in 'power', fifth letter 'O' is in 'honor' and 'vows', and last letter 'N' is the initial of 'end'.
altered_riddle: My first is in steel, sever bones and marrow. My second is in blade, forged in cold steel. My third is an arbalest, and also in arrows. My fourth is in spears, plunged through a shield. My fifth is in honor, and also in vows. My last will put an end to it all, and I am a time of year.
altered_reasoning: The alteration changes 'wield' to 'steel' (providing 'S'), changes 'power' to 'spears' (providing 'S'), and adds the constraint 'I am a time of year'. This yields S-E-A-S-O-N. 'Season' fits the new constraint and the acrostic, while 'weapon' fails the seasonal constraint.

original_riddle: Long and thin, red within, with a nail at the end.
original_reasoning: A finger is long and thin, has red flesh/blood within, and a fingernail at the tip.
altered_riddle: Long and thin, red within, with a nail at the end, found on the foot.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'found on the foot' eliminates 'finger' which is on the hand. A toe fits the physical description (long, thin, red flesh within, toenail at end) but is located on the foot.

original_riddle: I am always hungry, I must always be fed. The finger I lick will soon turn red.
original_reasoning: Fire consumes fuel ('hungry/fed') and burns whatever it touches, turning skin red and causing pain.
altered_riddle: I am always hungry, I must always be fed. The finger I bite will soon turn red.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'lick' to 'bite' eliminates fire, which burns rather than bites. Mosquitoes are 'always hungry' for blood. When they bite a finger, the histamine reaction causes the skin to turn red and itchy.

original_riddle: I pass before the sun, but make no shadow.
original_reasoning: Wind is invisible and intangible; it can flow between the observer and the sun without blocking light or casting a shadow.
altered_riddle: I pass before the sun, but make no shadow, and wet the earth.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'wet the earth' eliminates wind. Falling rain passes between the observer and the sun, but individual droplets are too small to cast visible shadows on the ground.

original_riddle: Forward I'm heavy, but backwards I'm not.
original_reasoning: The word 'ton' spelled forward is 'ton' (a unit of weight, implying heaviness). Spelled backwards, it becomes 'not' (negating heaviness).
altered_riddle: Forward I'm heavy, but backwards I'm nonsense.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'not' to 'nonsense' invalidates 'ton' because 'ton' backwards is 'not', which is a valid word, not nonsense. A 'stone' is physically heavy, and spelled backwards ('enots') it is nonsense.

original_riddle: I am two-faced but bear only one. I have no legs but travel widely. Men spill much blood over me. Kings leave their imprint on me. I have greatest power when given away, yet lust for me keeps me locked away.
original_reasoning: Coins are 'two-faced' (heads and tails) but usually bear a face on only one side. They travel through circulation. Blood is spilled over money. Kings are stamped on them. Money has power when spent, but greed (lust) keeps it hoarded.
altered_riddle: I am two-faced but bear only one. I have no legs but travel widely. Men spill much blood over me. Kings fear my imprint. I have greatest power when given away, yet lust for me keeps me locked away.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'leave their imprint on me' to 'fear my imprint' flips the power dynamic, invalidating the coin (which kings proudly stamp). A ballot is two-faced (paper has two sides) and bears a single mark. It travels through the democratic process. Blood is spilled in wars for rights. Kings (or tyrants) fear the 'imprint' of the vote. It has power when given (cast), but corruption (lust for power) keeps votes suppressed or 'locked away'.

original_riddle: At the sound of me, one may dream or stamp their feet, At the sound of me, one may laugh or sometimes weep.
original_reasoning: Music is heard (sound) and evokes various emotional and physical reactions like dreaming, dancing (stamp feet), laughing, or crying.
altered_riddle: At the touch of me, one may dream or stamp their feet, At the touch of me, one may laugh or sometimes weep.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'sound' to 'touch' eliminates music, which is auditory. Wind is felt (touch), can make one dream (cool breeze), stamp feet (cold reaction), or feel emotional, and is a physical force rather than an auditory art.

original_riddle: A box without hinges, lock or key, yet golden treasure lies within.
original_reasoning: An egg is shaped like a box (container) but has no mechanical openings like hinges or locks. The yolk inside is golden and is metaphorically the 'treasure'.
altered_riddle: A box without hinges, lock or key, yet golden treasure lies within, made of wax.
altered_reasoning: The constraint 'made of wax' rules out an egg (calcium carbonate shell). A honeycomb cell is a hexagonal box made of wax, contains no hinges or locks, and stores honey, which is golden treasure.

original_riddle: I have rivers without water. Forests without trees. Mountains without rocks. Towns without houses.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes representations of geographical features that lack physical substance. A map depicts these features symbolically without containing the actual materials.
altered_riddle: I have rivers without water. Forests without trees. Mountains without rocks. Towns without houses. And I tell a story.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'And I tell a story' eliminates maps, which are navigational tools rather than narrative devices. Books contain descriptions of landscapes and settings within their pages to convey a story.

original_riddle: What kind of room has no windows or doors?
original_reasoning: This is a pun on the suffix '-room'. A mushroom is a fungus, not a habitable space, and it naturally lacks architectural features like windows or doors.
altered_riddle: What kind of room has no windows or doors, and is often made by a car?
altered_reasoning: This alters the expectation of the word 'room' from a noun suffix denoting a space to an onomatopoeic suffix. 'Vroom' is the sound a car makes (a 'room' word), and as a sound, it has no windows or doors.

original_riddle: This thing all things devours: Birds, beasts, trees, flowers; Gnaws iron, bites steel; Grinds hard stones to meal; Slays king, ruins town, And beats high mountains down.
original_reasoning: Time erodes and ends everything eventually. It consumes life, corrodes metal, weathers stone, ends kingdoms, and erodes mountains through natural processes.
altered_riddle: This thing all things pushes: Birds, beasts, trees, flowers; Gnaws iron, bites steel; Grinds hard stones to meal; Slays king, ruins town, And beats high mountains down.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'devours' to 'pushes' shifts the meaning from consumption to physical force. Wind pushes clouds, birds, and trees; it erodes iron and stone via sandblast; and it shapes mountains through weathering.

original_riddle: My life is often a volume of grief, your help is needed to turn a new leaf. Stiff is my spine and my body is pale. But I'm always ready to tell a tale.
original_reasoning: A book contains stories (often of grief), requires the reader to turn pages (leaves), has a stiff spine, and has pale pages. It tells a story.
altered_riddle: My life is often a volume of grief, your help is needed to turn a new leaf. Stiff is my spine and my body is pale. I am born every morning, and I'm always ready to tell a tale.
altered_reasoning: The addition 'I am born every morning' introduces a lifecycle constraint that rules out a standard book. Newspapers are published daily (born every morning), contain news (often grief), have pages to turn, and tell tales of current events.

original_riddle: What is that over the head and under the hat?
original_reasoning: Hair grows on the surface of the head (over the head) and is covered when a hat is worn (under the hat).
altered_riddle: What is that over the head and under the hat, if you are bald?
altered_reasoning: The riddle counters the assumption that everyone has hair. If bald, the surface over the head and under the hat is the skin.

original_riddle: Metal or bone I may be, many teeth I have and always bared. Yet my bite harms no one. And ladies delight in my touch.
original_reasoning: Combs are often made of plastic, metal, or bone. They have 'teeth' that are always exposed ('bared'). They do not bite in a literal sense, and 'ladies delight' refers to the traditional use of combs for grooming hair.
altered_riddle: Metal or bone I may be, many teeth I have and always bared. Yet my bite harms no one. And I open every door.
altered_reasoning: Like a comb, a key can be made of metal or bone and has 'teeth' (the bitting) that are always bared. Its 'bite' does not harm anyone. The final line changes the function from grooming ('delight in my touch') to unlocking ('open every door'), which is the specific purpose of a key. A comb cannot open doors.

original_riddle: A warrior amongst the flowers, he bears a thrusting sword. He uses it whenever he must, to defend his golden hoard.
original_reasoning: The riddle describes a bee. 'Warrior amongst flowers' refers to its habitat and defensive nature. 'Thrusting sword' is its stinger. 'Golden hoard' is the honey it protects.
altered_riddle: A warrior amongst the flowers, he bears a thrusting sword. He uses it whenever he must, to defend his golden hoard, yet he is not alive.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'yet he is not alive' contradicts the biological assumption of a bee. A garden gnome is a stationary statue placed amongst flowers, often depicted holding a sword or staff, symbolically guarding the garden (hoard).

original_riddle: What runs around all day. Then lies under the bed. With its tongue hanging out?
original_reasoning: Shoes are worn while walking or running ('runs around'), are often stored under the bed, and have a fabric part called a 'tongue' that can hang out.
altered_riddle: What runs around all day. Then lies under the bed. With its tongue hanging out? It has a cord.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'It has a cord' eliminates 'shoe' as shoes do not have power cords. A vacuum cleaner moves around during cleaning ('runs'), is often stored under the bed, and has a power cord that hangs out.

original_riddle: I march before armies, a thousand salute me. My fall can bring victory, but no one would shoot me. The wind is my lover, one-legged am I. Name me and see me at home in the sky.
original_reasoning: Flags are carried before armies, saluted by soldiers, their capture (fall) signals victory, they are not shot at, they fly in the wind, stand on one pole (leg), and are high in the sky.
altered_riddle: I march before armies, a thousand salute me. My fall can bring victory, but no one would shoot me. The wind is my lover, formless am I. Name me and see me at home in the sky.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'one-legged' to 'formless' rules out the flag. Storm clouds march before armies (battle), are looked up to (salute), their fall (rain) brings victory (water), are not shot, love wind, are formless, and reside in the sky.

original_riddle: Runs smoother than any rhyme, loves to fall but cannot climb!
original_reasoning: Water flows (runs) smoothly, it falls as rain or in waterfalls, and it cannot move upward against gravity on its own.
altered_riddle: Runs smoother than any rhyme, loves to fall but cannot climb, and is found in a theater.
altered_reasoning: Water is generally not the defining feature of a theater. A curtain 'runs' along a track or rod, it 'falls' (drops) at the end of a scene, and it cannot climb back up without being pulled.

original_riddle: I drift forever with the current down these long canals they’ve made, Tame, yet wild, I run elusive Multitasking to your aid. Before I came, the world was darker, colder, sometimes, rougher, true. But though I might make living easy, I’m good at killing people too.
original_reasoning: Electricity flows through wires ('canals'), provides light and heat ('darker, colder' before it), powers many devices ('multitasking'), and can cause electrocution ('killing people').
altered_riddle: I drift forever with the current down these long canals they’ve made, Tame, yet wild, I run elusive Multitasking to your aid. Before I came, the world was darker, colder, sometimes, rougher, true. But though I might make living easy, I carry secrets people don’t know.
altered_reasoning: Changing the danger from physical death ('killing people') to information ('carry secrets') shifts the answer from electricity to the digital content flowing over it. Data flows through fibers/cables ('canals'), revolutionized life ('darker, colder' pre-internet communication), and carries private information. Electricity itself does not carry secrets.

original_riddle: What can fill a room but takes up no space?
original_reasoning: Light can illuminate a room without occupying any physical volume, so it fills the space without taking up space itself.
altered_riddle: What can fill a room in conversation but takes up no space?
altered_reasoning: The phrase ‘room in conversation’ shifts ‘room’ from a physical space to a figurative space of dialogue. Silence can fill that conversational space without occupying any physical space, while light, a physical phenomenon, does not fit this figurative context.

original_riddle: Voiceless it cries, Wingless flutters, Toothless bites, Mouthless mutters.
original_reasoning: The wind can howl (cry), move leaves or flags (flutter), feel cold and sharp (bite), and make murmuring sounds through obstacles (mutter), all without possessing the organs associated with those actions.
altered_riddle: Voiceless it cries, Wingless flutters, Toothless gnaws, Mouthless mutters.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'bites' to 'gnaws' shifts the metaphorical target. While wind bites, sorrow is famously described as 'gnawing' at the heart or mind. Sorrow fits the other clues (internal crying, fluttering heart, muttering thoughts) but is distinct from wind.

original_riddle: I am a box that holds keys without locks, yet they can unlock your soul.
original_reasoning: A piano is a large box-shaped instrument with keys that are not for locking doors; when played, the music is said to touch or 'unlock' the listener's soul.
altered_riddle: I am a box that holds keys without locks, yet they can unlock your soul. I have no hammers inside me.
altered_reasoning: Explicitly stating there are no hammers inside contradicts the essential mechanism of a piano (felt hammers striking strings). This forces identification of a keyboard instrument that produces sound without hammers: an organ, which uses air flowing through pipes or reeds.

original_riddle: What can fill a room but takes up no space?
original_reasoning: Light is a form of energy that can illuminate an entire room, filling it with brightness, yet it has no mass or physical volume, so it takes up no space.
altered_riddle: What can fill a room but takes up no space, and can be heard but not seen?
altered_reasoning: Sound travels through the air and fills the volume of a room, it is audible but invisible, and it does not occupy physical space. Light, by contrast, is visible, not audible, so it no longer fits the riddle.

original_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Under their emerald embrace…Either in their life or in their death.
original_reasoning: Trees are poetic entities of power and beauty. The 'emerald embrace' refers to their green leaf canopy, and 'grade' refers to the quality of their wood. They set the 'pace' of life through the changing seasons, and their wood remains useful ('in their death') for building or fuel.
altered_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to exist. Under their celestial embrace…Either in their life or in their death.
altered_reasoning: Changing 'emerald' to 'celestial' shifts the domain from Earth to space. Stars have 'grades' (stellar magnitudes and classifications) and set the pace of cosmic time and seasons. Changing 'live' to 'exist' reflects that all matter is formed in stars; when they 'die' in supernovae, they scatter the elements necessary for the universe to exist.

original_riddle: Without a bridle, or a saddle, across a thing I ride a-straddle. And those I ride, by help of me, though almost blind, are made to see.
original_reasoning: Glasses sit across the nose without a bridle or saddle, and they help people with poor vision to see.
altered_riddle: Without a bridle, or a saddle, across a mount I ride a-straddle. And those I ride, by help of me, though in the dark, are made to see.
altered_reasoning: The phrase 'in the dark' shifts the meaning from vision impairment to low light conditions. A spotlight is mounted on a stand or vehicle, straddling the mount, and it helps people see in the dark by providing light. Glasses do not provide light and are not typically mounted.

original_riddle: Voiceless it cries, Wingless flutters, Toothless bites, Mouthless mutters.
original_reasoning: Wind makes crying sounds, flutters things without wings, bites with its cold force, and mutters without a mouth.
altered_riddle: Voiceless it cries, Wingless flutters, Toothless bites, Mouthless mutters, and grows from the ground.
altered_reasoning: Wind cannot grow from the ground. A blade of grass is voiceless yet produces a crying rustle in the breeze, flutters winglessly, can deliver a tiny toothless bite or cut, mutters with a soft sound, and clearly grows from the ground.

original_riddle: Runs smoother than any rhyme, loves to fall but cannot climb!
original_reasoning: Water flows (runs) smoothly, it falls as rain or in waterfalls, and it cannot move upward against gravity on its own.
altered_riddle: Runs smoother than any rhyme, loves to fall but cannot climb, though it is not a liquid.
altered_reasoning: The original answer 'water' is explicitly ruled out by the phrase 'is not a liquid'. Snow 'runs' in drifts or avalanches, it falls from the sky, and it cannot climb.

original_riddle: I drift forever with the current down these long canals they’ve made, Tame, yet wild, I run elusive Multitasking to your aid. Before I came, the world was darker, colder, sometimes, rougher, true. But though I might make living easy, I’m good at killing people too.
original_reasoning: Electricity flows through wires ('canals'), provides light and heat ('darker, colder' before it), powers many devices ('multitasking'), and can cause electrocution ('killing people').
altered_riddle: I drift forever with the current down these long canals they’ve made, Tame, yet wild, I run elusive Multitasking to your aid. Before I came, the world was darker, colder, sometimes, rougher, true. But though I might make living easy, I am red and beat inside your chest too.
altered_reasoning: Adding 'red and beat inside your chest' completely rules out electricity. Blood flows through vessels ('canals they've made' interpreted as biological canals or veins), is vital for life (making living easy vs rough), can kill via hemorrhage or clots, and is red and pumped by the heart. Electricity is not red and does not beat.

original_riddle: What can fill a room but takes up no space?
original_reasoning: Light can illuminate a room without occupying any physical volume, so it fills the space without taking up space itself.
altered_riddle: What can fill a mind but takes up no space?
altered_reasoning: Thoughts can occupy the mental space of a mind without taking up any physical space, whereas light does not fill a mind in this sense. This swap changes the domain from physical to mental.

original_riddle: Voiceless it cries, Wingless flutters, Toothless bites, Mouthless mutters.
original_reasoning: The wind can howl (cry), move leaves or flags (flutter), feel cold and sharp (bite), and make murmuring sounds through obstacles (mutter), all without possessing the organs associated with those actions.
altered_riddle: Voiceless it cries, Wingless flutters, Toothless bites, Mouthless mutters, and has a spine but no bones.
altered_reasoning: A book fits the altered clues: it can make the reader cry (voiceless cries), its pages flutter (wingless flutters), its words can be biting (toothless bites), and it speaks to the reader (mouthless mutters). The final constraint 'has a spine but no bones' refers to the spine of a book binding, which definitively rules out wind.

original_riddle: I'm sometimes white and always wrong. I can break a heart and hurt the strong. I can build love or tear it down. I can make a smile or bring a frown.
original_reasoning: A 'white lie' is a minor untruth; lies are morally wrong; they can break hearts and hurt anyone; they can create false intimacy or destroy trust; they can make people happy (white lies) or sad.
altered_riddle: I'm sometimes white and always wrong. I can break a heart and hurt the strong. I can build love or tear it down. I can make a smile or bring a frown, and I'm swallowed to cure.
altered_reasoning: Adding the constraint of being medically 'swallowed to cure' rules out spoken/written lies. Placebos are often white pills; they are medically inert/wrong (no active ingredient); false hope can break hearts when revealed; they can build love through hope or destroy trust; they bring smiles (hope/relief) or frowns (ineffectiveness).

original_riddle: What can fill a room but takes up no space?
original_reasoning: Light is a form of energy that can illuminate an entire room, filling it with brightness, yet it has no mass or physical volume, so it takes up no space.
altered_riddle: What can darken a room but takes up no space?
altered_reasoning: Darkness is the absence of light; it can 'darken' a room, filling it with darkness, and it is intangible, occupying no space. Light cannot darken a room, so the original answer is ruled out.

original_riddle: Creatures of power, creatures of grade, creatures of beauty, creatures of strength. As for their lives, they set everything's pace. For all things must come to live. Under their emerald embrace…Either in their life or in their death.
original_reasoning: Trees are poetic entities of power and beauty. The 'emerald embrace' refers to their green leaf canopy, and 'grade' refers to the quality of their wood. They set the 'pace' of life through the changing seasons, and their wood remains useful ('in their death') for building or fuel.
